54	# Blueprint Writer
55	
56	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
57	
58	## Your Job
59	
60	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
61	
62	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
63	
64	## Directive Format
65	
66	```markdown
67	# Blueprint Writer Directive
68	
69	## Slug
70	<slug>
71	
72	## Target chapter
73	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
74	
75	## Strategy context
76	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
77	
78	## Required content
79	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
80	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
81	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
82	- ...
83	
84	## Out of scope
85	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
86	
87	## References
88	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
89	- ...
90	
91	## Expected outcome
92	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
93	```
94	
95	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
96	
97	## Chapter format
98	
99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
107	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
108	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
109	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
110	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
111	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
112	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
113	\end{theorem}
114	
115	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
116	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
117	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
118	\begin{proof}
119	  \uses{thm:another_result}
120	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
121	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
122	\end{proof}
123	```
124	
125	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
126	
127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139	
140	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
147	
148	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
149	
150	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
151	
152	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
153	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
154	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
155	
156	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. 72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74

2	
3	\chapter{Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II \S6, pp.~130--137 +
7	% IV.1, pp.~294--296 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
8	% Stacks pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (divisors),
9	% 02ME (order at a point on a regular 1-dimensional scheme),
10	% 0BE0 (degree of a divisor on a curve), 0BE3 (principal divisors have
11	% degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).
12	
13	\section{Setup and motivation}
14	
15	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
16	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
17	\[
18	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
19	  \,\colon\,
20	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
21	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
22	\]
23	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
24	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
25	Riemann--Roch dimension formula $\ell(D) = \deg D + 1$ for $\deg D \geq 0$ on
26	a genus-$0$ curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
27	$\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ via $1$ and a non-constant function
28	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve $\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1$''
29	classification recovered as a degree-$1$ morphism into $\mathbb P^1$ via
30	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
31	
32	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
33	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
34	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
35	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
36	codimension-$1$ cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
37	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
38	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
39	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
40	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
41	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
42	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
43	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
44	The audit grounding this scope decision is in
45	\texttt{analogies/rrbridge-survey.md}.
46	
47	This chapter establishes the formal-sum data type
48	$\Div(X) = \bigoplus_{\substack{Z \subset X \\ \codim 1}} \mathbb Z$ on a
49	Noetherian integral scheme $X$ satisfying Hartshorne's condition $(*)$, the
50	principal-divisor homomorphism $\div \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \Div(X)$ on a
51	curve, the degree map $\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z$ on a smooth proper
52	curve over an algebraically closed field, and the linear-equivalence
53	relation. The \emph{degree-zero of a principal divisor} statement
54	(\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}) is included for completeness; its proof
55	decomposes into a separate sub-build inside \texttt{RR.1} (it uses the
56	auxiliary lemma that any non-constant rational function on $C$ defines a
57	finite morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1$). The line-bundle construction $\mathcal
58	O_C(D)$ and the associated cohomology computation $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
59	live in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3}; the Riemann--Roch dimension
60	formula lives in \texttt{RR.2}; the genus-$0$ classification proper lives in
61	\texttt{RR.4}.
62	
63	Throughout this chapter, the standing convention is Hartshorne's condition
64	$(*)$:
65	
66	% SOURCE QUOTE: "$(\ast)$ $X$ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which
67	% is regular in codimension one."
68	\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130, the conditional $(*)$.}
69	\[
70	  (\ast)\qquad
71	  X \text{ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which is regular in
72	  codimension one.}
73	\]
74	For the degree map and for principal-divisor statements on a curve, we
75	additionally assume $X$ is a complete (i.e.\ proper) nonsingular curve over
76	an algebraically closed field $\bar k$, where Hartshorne's condition $(*)$ is
77	automatic.
78	
79	\section{Codim-$1$ cycle group}
80	
81	\begin{definition}
82	
83	\leanok
84	[Codim-$1$ cycle group / Weil divisor group of a scheme]
85	  \label{def:codim1_cycles}
86	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor}
87	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "prime divisor" and
88	  % "Weil divisor" under condition $(*)$) (read from
89	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 147).
90	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02RW.
91	  % NOTE (iter-172): the Lean encoding factors via a helper structure
92	  % \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor} (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:84-90})
93	  % whose codimension-1-and-integral witness field is currently a placeholder
94	  % \texttt{isCodim1AndIntegral : True := trivial}, awaiting iter-173+ refinement
95	  % to the genuine \texttt{Order.coheight x = 1 \(\wedge\) IsIntegral (closedSubschemeOfPoint x)}
96	  % conjunction. \emph{Consequence:} until that refinement lands, \texttt{Scheme.WeilDivisor X} is
97	  % structurally the free abelian group on \emph{all} points of $X$ rather than on codim-1
98	  % integral subschemes; downstream RR-side consumers must wait. (Flagged by iter-172
99	  % lean-auditor as must-fix-before-any-sibling-RR-file-consumes-PrimeDivisor.)
100	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$. A \emph{prime
101	  % divisor} on $X$ is a closed integral subscheme $Y$ of codimension one.
102	  % A \emph{Weil divisor} is an element of the free abelian group
103	  % $\operatorname{Div} X$ generated by the prime divisors. We write a
104	  % divisor as $D = \sum n_i Y_i$, where the $Y_i$ are prime divisors, the
105	  % $n_i$ are integers, and only finitely many $n_i$ are different from
106	  % zero. If all the $n_i \geq 0$, we say that $D$ is \emph{effective}."
107	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130.}
108	  Let $X$ be a Noetherian integral separated scheme that is regular in
109	  codimension one (i.e.\ $X$ satisfies $(*)$). A \emph{prime divisor} on
110	  $X$ is a closed integral subscheme $Y \subset X$ of codimension one. The
111	  \emph{Weil divisor group}
112	  \[
113	    \Div(X) \;=\; \bigoplus_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
114	    \mathbb Z \cdot Y
115	  \]
116	  is the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of $X$; an element
117	  $D = \sum n_i Y_i$ with finitely many nonzero coefficients $n_i \in
118	  \mathbb Z$ is called a \emph{Weil divisor}, and is \emph{effective} when
119	  all $n_i \geq 0$.
120	\end{definition}
121	
122	\section{Order of a rational function at a prime divisor}
123	
124	\begin{definition}
125	
126	\leanok
127	[Order of a rational function at a prime divisor]
128	  \label{def:order_at_point}
129	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order}
130	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
131	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130--131 (valuation $v_Y$ of a prime
132	  % divisor; "zero/pole of $f$ along $Y$") (read from
133	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
134	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02ME.
135	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If $Y$ is a prime divisor on $X$, let $\eta \in Y$ be its
136	  % generic point. Then the local ring $\mathcal O_{\eta, X}$ of $X$ at
137	  % $\eta$ is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field $K$, the
138	  % function field of $X$. We call the corresponding discrete valuation
139	  % $v_Y$ the \emph{valuation of $Y$}."
140	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let $f \in K^\ast$ be any nonzero rational function
141	  % on $X$. Then $v_Y(f)$ is an integer. If it is positive, we say $f$ has
142	  % a \emph{zero} along $Y$, of that order; if it is negative, we say $f$
143	  % has a \emph{pole} along $Y$, of order $-v_Y(f)$."
144	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation $v_Y$).}
145	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. For a prime divisor $Y \subset X$ with generic
146	  point $\eta \in Y$, the local ring $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$ is a discrete
147	  valuation ring (by regularity in codimension one) with quotient field the
148	  function field $K(X)$. For a nonzero rational function $f \in K(X)^{\times}$
149	  the \emph{order of $f$ along $Y$} is the integer
150	  \[
151	    \ord_Y(f) \;:=\; v_Y(f) \;\in\; \mathbb Z,
152	  \]
153	  where $v_Y$ is the normalised discrete valuation associated to
154	  $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$. If $\ord_Y(f) > 0$, $f$ has a \emph{zero of
155	  order $\ord_Y(f)$ along $Y$}; if $\ord_Y(f) < 0$, $f$ has a \emph{pole
156	  of order $-\ord_Y(f)$ along $Y$}.
157	
158	  Specialisation to a curve. If $C$ is a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$
159	  and $P \in C$ is a closed point, then $P$ is automatically a prime
160	  divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the $1$-dimensional
161	  scheme $C$); the local ring $\mathcal O_{C, P}$ is a discrete valuation
162	  ring by smoothness, and $\ord_P(f) = v_P(f)$ is the standard DVR
163	  valuation of $f$ at $P$.
164	\end{definition}
165	
166	\section{Divisor of a closed point on a curve}
167	
168	\begin{definition}
169	
170	\leanok
171	[Divisor of a closed point on a curve]
172	  \label{def:divisor_closed_point}
173	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint}
174	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
175	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
176	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154):
177	  % "A prime divisor is just a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can
178	  % be written $D = \sum n_i P_i$, where the $P_i$ are closed points, and
179	  % $n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
180	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now we come to the study of divisors on curves. If $X$
181	  % is a nonsingular curve, then $X$ satisfies the condition $(\ast)$ used
182	  % above, so we can talk about divisors on $X$. A prime divisor is just
183	  % a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can be written $D = \sum n_i
184	  % P_i$, where the $P_i$ are closed points, and $n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
185	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (``Divisors on Curves'').}
186	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over a field $k$ and let $P \in C$ be a
187	  closed point. The \emph{Weil divisor associated to $P$} is the element
188	  \[
189	    [P] \;:=\; 1 \cdot P \;\in\; \Div(C)
190	  \]
191	  i.e.\ the prime divisor $P$ with multiplicity one. The assignment is
192	  well-defined because the closed point $P$ is automatically a codimension-$1$
193	  integral closed subscheme of the $1$-dimensional integral scheme $C$.
194	  Conversely, every prime divisor on $C$ is of this form, so an arbitrary
195	  divisor on $C$ is a finite formal $\mathbb Z$-linear combination
196	  $\sum n_i [P_i]$ of closed points.
197	\end{definition}
198	
199	\section{Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base}
200	
201	\begin{definition}
202	
203	\leanok
204	[Degree of a divisor on a curve over $\bar k$]
205	  \label{def:divisor_degree}
206	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree}
207	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:divisor_closed_point}
208	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
209	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
210	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE0.
211	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We define the \emph{degree} of $D$ to be $\sum n_i$."
212	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (definition of degree on a
213	  nonsingular curve).}
214	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
215	  $\bar k$. The \emph{degree} of a Weil divisor $D = \sum n_i [P_i] \in
216	  \Div(C)$ is the integer
217	  \[
218	    \deg(D) \;:=\; \sum_i n_i \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
219	  \]
220	  The definition uses that every closed point of $C$ has residue field
221	  $\bar k$ (so each prime divisor contributes degree one); over a general
222	  field $k$ the same definition with the residue-field-degree weight
223	  $\deg(D) := \sum_i n_i \, [\kappa(P_i) : k]$ recovers the
224	  \emph{geometric} degree, but the project's RR bridge needs only the
225	  $\bar k$ specialisation.
226	\end{definition}
227	
228	\begin{theorem}
229	
230	\leanok
231	[The degree map is a group homomorphism]
232	  \label{thm:divisor_degree_hom}
233	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_hom}
234	  \uses{def:divisor_degree}
235	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
236	  $\bar k$. The degree map of \Cref{def:divisor_degree} is a group
237	  homomorphism
238	  \[
239	    \deg \colon \Div(C) \longrightarrow \mathbb Z,
240	    \qquad
241	    \deg(D_1 + D_2) \;=\; \deg(D_1) + \deg(D_2),
242	    \qquad
243	    \deg(-D) \;=\; -\deg(D).
244	  \]
245	\end{theorem}
246	
247	\begin{proof}
248	  \uses{def:divisor_degree}
249	  Immediate from the definition: $\Div(C)$ is the free abelian group on the
250	  set of closed points of $C$, and $\deg$ is the unique group homomorphism
251	  sending each generator $[P]$ to $1 \in \mathbb Z$ (equivalently: the sum
252	  of the multiplicities). Both additivity and negation thus reduce to the
253	  corresponding identities for finite sums of integers.
254	\end{proof}
255	
256	\section{Principal divisors}
257	
258	\begin{definition}
259	
260	\leanok
261	[Principal divisor of a rational function]
262	  \label{def:principal_divisor}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal}
264	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:order_at_point}
265	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Lemma~6.1 + the definition of $\operatorname{div}(f) = (f)$,
266	  % p.~131 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
267	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma 6.1. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$, and let $f \in K^\ast$
268	  % be a nonzero function on $X$. Then $v_Y(f) = 0$ for all except finitely
269	  % many prime divisors $Y$."
270	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$ and let $f \in K^\ast$.
271	  % We define the \emph{divisor of $f$}, denoted $(f)$, by
272	  % $(f) = \sum v_Y(f) \cdot Y$, where the sum is taken over all prime
273	  % divisors $Y$ of $X$. By the lemma, this is a finite sum, hence is a
274	  % divisor. Any divisor which is equal to the divisor of a function is
275	  % called a \emph{principal divisor}."
276	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Lemma~6.1 and the following definition,
277	  p.~131.}
278	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. For a nonzero rational function $f \in K(X)^{\times}$,
279	  Hartshorne's Lemma~6.1 asserts that $\ord_Y(f) = 0$ for all but finitely
280	  many prime divisors $Y$ on $X$; hence the formal sum
281	  \[
282	    \div(f) \;:=\; \sum_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
283	    \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y \;\in\; \Div(X)
284	  \]
285	  has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor, called the
286	  \emph{principal divisor of $f$}. On a smooth proper curve $C$ over $\bar k$
287	  the same definition specialises to
288	  $\div(f) = \sum_{P \in C \text{ closed}} \ord_P(f) \cdot [P]$, the formal
289	  sum over closed points.
290	\end{definition}
291	
292	\begin{theorem}
293	
294	\leanok
295	[The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism]
296	  \label{thm:principal_hom}
297	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_hom}
298	  \uses{def:principal_divisor, def:order_at_point}
299	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, the paragraph after the principal-divisor
300	  % definition, p.~131 (read from
301	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
302	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Note that if $f, g \in K^\ast$, then $(f/g) = (f) - (g)$
303	  % because of the properties of valuations. Therefore sending a function
304	  % $f$ to its divisor $(f)$ gives a homomorphism of the multiplicative
305	  % group $K^\ast$ to the additive group $\operatorname{Div} X$, and the
306	  % image, which consists of the principal divisors, is a subgroup of
307	  % $\operatorname{Div} X$."
308	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (group-homomorphism remark).}
309	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. The principal-divisor map
310	  $\div \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \Div(X)$ is a group homomorphism from the
311	  multiplicative group of nonzero rational functions to the additive group
312	  of Weil divisors. Concretely
313	  \[
314	    \div(fg) \;=\; \div(f) + \div(g),
315	    \qquad
316	    \div(f^{-1}) \;=\; -\div(f),
317	    \qquad
318	    \div(1) \;=\; 0.
319	  \]
320	\end{theorem}
321	
322	\begin{proof}
323	  \uses{def:principal_divisor, def:order_at_point}
324	  Each prime divisor $Y$ on $X$ contributes a discrete valuation $v_Y$ on
325	  $K(X)$ (\Cref{def:order_at_point}); by the standard DVR axioms,
326	  $v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)$ and $v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)$ for $f, g \in
327	  K(X)^{\times}$, and $v_Y(1) = 0$. Summing these identities over all prime
328	  divisors of $X$ (a sum that has finite support on each factor by
329	  \Cref{def:principal_divisor}, so the support of the sum is the union
330	  of the two finite supports and remains finite) gives the three displayed
331	  identities.
332	\end{proof}
333	
334	\begin{theorem}
335	
336	\leanok
337	[Principal divisors have degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve]
338	  \label{thm:principal_deg_zero}
339	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero}
340	  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:principal_divisor, thm:principal_hom}
341	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138 (read from
342	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 155).
343	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE3.
344	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.10. \emph{A principal divisor on a complete
345	  % nonsingular curve $X$ has degree zero. Consequently the degree function
346	  % induces a surjective homomorphism $\deg \colon \operatorname{Cl} X \to
347	  % \mathbf Z$.}"
348	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138.}
349	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
350	  $\bar k$. For every nonzero rational function $f \in K(C)^{\times}$ the
351	  associated principal divisor has degree zero:
352	  \[
353	    \deg(\div(f)) \;=\; 0 \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
354	  \]
355	\end{theorem}
356	
357	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Let $f \in K(X)^\ast$. If $f \in k$, then
358	% $(f) = 0$, so there is nothing to prove. If $f \notin k$, then the
359	% inclusion of fields $k(f) \subset K(X)$ induces a finite morphism
360	% $\varphi \colon X \to \mathbf P^1$. It is a morphism by (I, 6.12). We
361	% have $(f) = \varphi^\ast(\{0\} - \{\infty\})$. Since $\{0\} - \{\infty\}$
362	% is a divisor of degree 0 on $\mathbf P^1$, we conclude that $(f)$ has
363	% degree 0 on $X$."
364	\begin{proof}
365	  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:principal_divisor, thm:principal_hom}
366	  Following Hartshorne's Corollary~6.10. If $f \in \bar k \subset K(C)$ is
367	  a constant scalar, then $f$ has no zeros or poles, so $\div(f) = 0$ and
368	  $\deg(\div(f)) = 0$. Otherwise the inclusion of fields
369	  $\bar k(f) \subset K(C)$ exhibits $K(C)$ as a finite extension of the
370	  rational function field $\bar k(f) \cong \bar k(t)$, so the corresponding
371	  morphism $\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ is finite (by the
372	  smooth-proper-curve / function-field correspondence on which a curve is
373	  determined by its function field, classical I.6.12). The principal
374	  divisor $\div(f)$ equals the pullback $\varphi^{*}([0] - [\infty])$
375	  along $\varphi$ of the degree-$0$ divisor $[0] - [\infty]$ on
376	  $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$; since pullback along a finite morphism
377	  multiplies degrees by $\deg(\varphi)$, we conclude
378	  $\deg(\div(f)) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg([0] - [\infty]) = \deg(\varphi)
379	  \cdot 0 = 0$.
380	
381	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The two auxiliary statements ``the inclusion
382	  $\bar k(f) \subset K(C)$ defines a finite morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1$''
383	  and ``pullback along a finite morphism of curves multiplies degrees by
384	  the degree of the morphism'' (Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.9, the
385	  multiplicativity of degree) are themselves separable sub-lemmas of
386	  \texttt{RR.1}; they are stated here for completeness and slotted to be
387	  closed in a follow-up iteration. The signature
388	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal\_degree\_zero}
389	  is recorded as the public face of this theorem and is depended on by
390	  the \texttt{RR.3}/\texttt{RR.4} chain.
391	\end{proof}
392	
393	\section{Divisor class group}
394	
395	We follow Hartshorne II.6 (pp.~131--132) and define the divisor class group
396	of a curve as the cokernel of the principal-divisor homomorphism. This is
397	the \emph{Picard group} of $C$ in the curve-specific sense; the line-bundle
398	interpretation $\Cl(C) \cong \Pic(C)$ requires the construction of
399	$\mathcal O_C(D)$, which lives in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3} and is
400	out of scope here.
401	
402	\begin{definition}
403	
404	\leanok
405	[Linear equivalence of divisors]
406	  \label{def:linear_equivalence}
407	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.LinearEquivalence}
408	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:principal_divisor, thm:principal_hom}
409	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~131 (definition of linear equivalence
410	  % and of $\operatorname{Cl} X$) (read from
411	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
412	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$. Two divisors $D$
413	  % and $D'$ are said to be \emph{linearly equivalent}, written $D \sim D'$,
414	  % if $D - D'$ is a principal divisor. The group $\operatorname{Div} X$ of
415	  % all divisors divided by the subgroup of principal divisors is called
416	  % the \emph{divisor class group} of $X$, and is denoted by
417	  % $\operatorname{Cl} X$."
418	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (linear equivalence and
419	  $\operatorname{Cl} X$).}
420	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. Two Weil divisors $D, D' \in \Div(X)$ are
421	  \emph{linearly equivalent}, written $D \sim D'$, if and only if there
422	  exists $f \in K(X)^{\times}$ with
423	  \[
424	    D - D' \;=\; \div(f) \;\in\; \Div(X).
425	  \]
426	  The relation $\sim$ is an equivalence relation on $\Div(X)$ (the
427	  reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity all transport from the analogous
428	  identities $\div(1) = 0$, $\div(f^{-1}) = -\div(f)$, $\div(fg) =
429	  \div(f) + \div(g)$ via \Cref{thm:principal_hom}); the corresponding
430	  quotient group $\Cl(X) := \Div(X) / \mathrm{im}(\div)$ is the
431	  \emph{divisor class group} of $X$. On a smooth proper curve $C$ over
432	  $\bar k$, \Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero} implies that the degree map
433	  $\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z$ descends to a homomorphism
434	  $\deg \colon \Cl(C) \to \mathbb Z$.
435	\end{definition}
436

451	    (sibling chapter \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RRFormula.tex}, to be added).

455	    (sibling chapter \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added)

463	  \item \emph{Higher-codimension cycles} (the Chow group
464	    $\bigoplus_i \mathrm{CH}^i(X)$) are out of scope: this chapter is
465	    strictly curve-focused.
466	  \item The proof of \Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero} factors through two
467	    auxiliary statements (``finite morphism induced by a non-constant

470	    sub-lemmas of \texttt{RR.1}; they are flagged in the proof body but
471	    deliberately not unfolded as standalone declarations here.
472	\end{itemize}

110	/-! ## §2. Order of a rational function at a prime divisor
111	
112	For a scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, every prime divisor `Y` carries a discrete
113	valuation `v_Y` on the function field `K(X)`. The order of a nonzero rational

126	divisor and `ord_P(f) = v_P(f)` is the standard DVR valuation at `P`.

131	`addVal` (`IsDiscreteValuationRing.addVal : R → ℕ∞`), extends it to the fraction field

133	noncomputable def order {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)

143	/-! ## §3. Divisor of a closed point on a curve
144	
145	On a smooth proper curve `C` over a field, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
146	divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the one-dimensional integral
147	scheme `C`). The associated Weil divisor is `[P] = 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`. -/
148	
149	/-- **The Weil divisor associated to a closed point `P` on a curve.** The element
150	`[P] := 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`, i.e. the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one.
151	
152	For a smooth proper curve every closed point is automatically a prime divisor (it
153	is a codimension-one integral closed subscheme of the one-dimensional integral
154	scheme `C`); conversely every prime divisor on a curve is of this form, so an
155	arbitrary divisor on `C` is a finite formal `ℤ`-linear combination
156	`Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]` of closed points.
157	
158	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_closed_point` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137).
159	
160	iter-173+: the body promotes the closed point `P` to a `PrimeDivisor` (using the
161	curve-specific closed-point ↔ prime-divisor bijection) and emits
162	`Finsupp.single _ 1`. -/

164	    (_hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) : C.WeilDivisor :=
165	  sorry

167	/-! ## §4. Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base -/
168	
169	/-- **Degree of a Weil divisor on a smooth proper curve over `k̄`.**
170	
171	Over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, every closed point of a smooth proper
172	curve `C` has residue field `k̄`, so each prime divisor `[P]` contributes degree
173	one to the sum, and `deg(D) := Σᵢ nᵢ` is the sum of the integer coefficients of
174	the formal sum `D = Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]`.

176	(Over a general field `k` one weights by the residue-field degrees
177	`Σᵢ nᵢ · [κ(Pᵢ) : k]` to recover the geometric degree; the project's RR bridge

180	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_degree` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137; Stacks 0BE0).
181

183	the finitely supported function representing `D`. -/
184	noncomputable def degree (D : X.WeilDivisor) : ℤ :=
185	  (D : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ).sum (fun _ n => n)

188	
189	`deg(D₁ + D₂) = deg(D₁) + deg(D₂)`, `deg(-D) = -deg(D)`, `deg(0) = 0`. Bundled
190	as an `AddMonoidHom` for downstream use (the linear-equivalence quotient

193	Blueprint reference: `thm:divisor_degree_hom` (immediate from `def:divisor_degree`).

196	hom `fun _ n => n`) + `Finsupp.sum_zero_index` (no support ⇒ empty sum). -/
197	noncomputable def degree_hom : X.WeilDivisor →+ ℤ :=
198	  sorry

200	/-! ## §5. Principal divisors -/
201	
202	/-- **The principal divisor of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×`.**
203	
204	By Hartshorne's Lemma 6.1, `ord_Y(f) = 0` for all but finitely many prime
205	divisors `Y`, so the formal sum
206	`div(f) := Σ_{Y prime divisor} ord_Y(f) · Y ∈ Div(X)`
207	has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor.
208	
209	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, this specialises to
210	`div(f) = Σ_{P closed point} ord_P(f) · [P]`.
211	
212	Blueprint reference: `def:principal_divisor` (Hartshorne II §6 Lemma 6.1 +
213	following definition, p. 131).

215	iter-173+: the body invokes Hartshorne 6.1 for the finite-support side condition,
216	then materialises the `Finsupp` whose value at each prime divisor `Y` is
217	`Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f`. -/
218	noncomputable def principal [IsIntegral X] (f : X.functionField)
219	    (_hf : f ≠ 0) : X.WeilDivisor :=
220	  sorry
221	
222	/-- **The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism `K(X)^× → Div(X)`.**
223	
224	Concretely `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, `div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, `div(1) = 0`. Bundled
225	as a `MonoidHom` from the multiplicative units of `K(X)` to `Multiplicative (Div(X))`
226	(equivalently: an additive map `(K(X)^×, ·) → (Div(X), +)`).
227	
228	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_hom` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131).
229

231	`v_Y(f⁻¹) = -v_Y(f)`, `v_Y(1) = 0` (`AddValuation.map_add`, etc.), summing over the
232	union of supports. -/
233	noncomputable def principal_hom [IsIntegral X] :
234	    (X.functionField)ˣ →* Multiplicative X.WeilDivisor :=
235	  sorry

237	/-- **Principal divisors on a complete nonsingular curve have degree zero**
238	(Hartshorne Corollary II.6.10, Stacks 0BE3).
239	
240	For every nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(C)^×` on a smooth proper curve `C`
241	over an algebraically closed field `k̄`,
242	`deg(div(f)) = 0 ∈ ℤ`.
243	
244	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_deg_zero` (Hartshorne II.6 Cor. 6.10 p. 138).
245	
246	The proof (Hartshorne 6.10): if `f ∈ k̄` is constant then `div(f) = 0` and the
247	claim is trivial. Otherwise the inclusion `k̄(f) ⊂ K(C)` exhibits `K(C)` as a
248	finite extension of `k̄(f) ≅ k̄(t)`, so the corresponding morphism
249	`φ : C → ℙ¹_{k̄}` is finite, `div(f) = φ^*([0] - [∞])`, and pullback along a
250	finite morphism multiplies degree by `deg(φ)`. Two auxiliary sub-lemmas
251	(finite morphism induced by a non-constant rational function; multiplicativity
252	of degree under finite pullback, Hartshorne II.6.9) are deferred to follow-up
253	iters of `RR.1`. -/
254	theorem principal_degree_zero {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
255	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
256	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
257	    [IsIntegral C.left] (f : C.left.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0) :
258	    degree (principal f hf) = 0 :=
259	  sorry
260	
261	/-! ## §6. Linear equivalence and the divisor class group -/
262	
263	/-- **Linear equivalence of Weil divisors.**
264	
265	Two Weil divisors `D, D' ∈ Div(X)` are linearly equivalent, written `D ~ D'`, if
266	and only if there exists a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` with
267	`D - D' = div(f) ∈ Div(X)`.
268	
269	`~` is an equivalence relation (reflexivity from `div(1) = 0`, symmetry from
270	`div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, transitivity from `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, all via
271	`thm:principal_hom`). The quotient `Cl(X) := Div(X) / im(div)` is the
272	**divisor class group** of `X`.
273	
274	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, `thm:principal_deg_zero` shows the
275	degree map descends to `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ`.
276	
277	Blueprint reference: `def:linear_equivalence` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131). -/
278	def LinearEquivalence [IsIntegral X] (D D' : X.WeilDivisor) : Prop :=
279	  ∃ (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0), D - D' = principal f hf
280	
281	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
282	
283	end AlgebraicGeometry

/-!
# Krull dimension of a preordered set and height of an element

If `α` is a preordered set, then `krullDim α : WithBot ℕ∞` is defined to be
`sup {n | a₀ < a₁ < ... < aₙ}`.

In case that `α` is empty, then its Krull dimension is defined to be negative infinity; if the
length of all series `a₀ < a₁ < ... < aₙ` is unbounded, then its Krull dimension is defined to be
positive infinity.

For `a : α`, its height (in `ℕ∞`) is defined to be `sup {n | a₀ < a₁ < ... < aₙ ≤ a}`, while its
coheight is defined to be `sup {n | a ≤ a₀ < a₁ < ... < aₙ}` .

## Main results

* The Krull dimension is the same as that of the dual order (`krullDim_orderDual`).

* The Krull dimension is the supremum of the heights of the elements (`krullDim_eq_iSup_height`),
  or their coheights (`krullDim_eq_iSup_coheight`), or their sums of height and coheight
  (`krullDim_eq_iSup_height_add_coheight_of_nonempty`)

* The height in the dual order equals the coheight, and vice versa.

* The height is monotone (`height_mono`), and strictly monotone if finite (`height_strictMono`).

* The coheight is antitone (`coheight_anti`), and strictly antitone if finite
  (`coheight_strictAnti`).

* The height is the supremum of the successor of the height of all smaller elements
  (`height_eq_iSup_lt_height`).

* The elements of height zero are the minimal elements (`height_eq_zero`), and the elements of
  height `n` are minimal among those of height `≥ n` (`height_eq_coe_iff_minimal_le_height`).

* Concrete calculations for the height, coheight and Krull dimension in `ℕ`, `ℤ`, `WithTop`,
  `WithBot` and `ℕ∞`.

## Design notes

Krull dimensions are defined to take value in `WithBot ℕ∞` so that `(-∞) + (+∞)` is
also negative infinity. This is because we want Krull dimensions to be additive with respect
to product of varieties so that `-∞` being the Krull dimension of empty variety is equal to
sum of `-∞` and the Krull dimension of any other varieties.

We could generalize the notion of Krull dimension to an arbitrary binary relation; many results
in this file would generalize as well. But we don't think it would be useful, so we only define
Krull dimension of a preorder.
-/

@[expose] public section

assert_not_exists Field

namespace Order

section definitions

/--
The **Krull dimension** of a preorder `α` is the supremum of the rightmost index of all relation
series of `α` ordered by `<`. If there is no series `a₀ < a₁ < ... < aₙ` in `α`, then its Krull
dimension is defined to be negative infinity; if the length of all series `a₀ < a₁ < ... < aₙ` is
unbounded, its Krull dimension is defined to be positive infinity.
-/
noncomputable def krullDim (α : Type*) [Preorder α] : WithBot ℕ∞ :=
  ⨆ (p : LTSeries α), p.length

/--
The **height** of an element `a` in a preorder `α` is the supremum of the rightmost index of all
relation series of `α` ordered by `<` and ending below or at `a`. In other words, it is
the largest `n` such that there's a series `a₀ < a₁ < ... < aₙ = a` (or `∞` if there is
no largest `n`).
-/
noncomputable def height {α : Type*} [Preorder α] (a : α) : ℕ∞ :=
  ⨆ (p : LTSeries α) (_ : p.last ≤ a), p.length

/--
The **coheight** of an element `a` in a preorder `α` is the supremum of the rightmost index of all
relation series of `α` ordered by `<` and beginning with `a`. In other words, it is
the largest `n` such that there's a series `a = a₀ < a₁ < ... < aₙ` (or `∞` if there is
no largest `n`).

The definition of `coheight` is via the `height` in the dual order, in order to easily transfer
theorems between `height` and `coheight`. See `coheight_eq` for the definition with a
series ordered by `<` and beginning with `a`.
-/
noncomputable def coheight {α : Type*} [Preorder α] (a : α) : ℕ∞ := height (α := αᵒᵈ) a

end definitions

/-!
## Height
-/

section height

variable {α β : Type*}

variable [Preorder α] [Preorder β]

@[simp] lemma height_toDual (x : α) : height (OrderDual.toDual x) = coheight x := rfl
@[simp] lemma height_ofDual (x : αᵒᵈ) : height (OrderDual.ofDual x) = coheight x := rfl
@[simp] lemma coheight_toDual (x : α) : coheight (OrderDual.toDual x) = height x := rfl
@[simp] lemma coheight_ofDual (x : αᵒᵈ) : coheight (OrderDual.ofDual x) = height x := rfl

/--
The **coheight** of an element `a` in a preorder `α` is the supremum of the rightmost index of all
relation series of `α` ordered by `<` and beginning with `a`.

This is not the definition of `coheight`. The definition of `coheight` is via the `height` in the
dual order, in order to easily transfer theorems between `height` and `coheight`.
-/
lemma coheight_eq (a : α) :
    coheight a = ⨆ (p : LTSeries α) (_ : a ≤ p.head), (p.length : ℕ∞) := by
  apply Equiv.iSup_congr ⟨RelSeries.reverse, RelSeries.reverse, fun _ ↦ RelSeries.reverse_reverse _,
    fun _ ↦ RelSeries.reverse_reverse _⟩
  congr! 1

lemma height_le_iff {a : α} {n : ℕ∞} :
    height a ≤ n ↔ ∀ ⦃p : LTSeries α⦄, p.last ≤ a → p.length ≤ n := by
  rw [height, iSup₂_le_iff]

lemma coheight_le_iff {a : α} {n : ℕ∞} :
    coheight a ≤ n ↔ ∀ ⦃p : LTSeries α⦄, a ≤ p.head → p.length ≤ n := by
  rw [coheight_eq, iSup₂_le_iff]

lemma height_le {a : α} {n : ℕ∞} (h : ∀ (p : LTSeries α), p.last = a → p.length ≤ n) :
    height a ≤ n := by
  apply height_le_iff.mpr
  intro p hlast
  wlog hlenpos : p.length ≠ 0
  · simp_all
  -- We replace the last element in the series with `a`
  let p' := p.eraseLast.snoc a (lt_of_lt_of_le (p.eraseLast_last_rel_last (by simp_all)) hlast)
  rw [show p.length = p'.length by simp [p']; lia]
  apply h
  simp [p']

/--
Variant of `height_le_iff` ranging only over those series that end exactly on `a`.
-/
lemma height_le_iff' {a : α} {n : ℕ∞} :
    height a ≤ n ↔ ∀ ⦃p : LTSeries α⦄, p.last = a → p.length ≤ n := by
  constructor
  · rw [height_le_iff]
    exact fun h p hlast => h (le_of_eq hlast)
  · exact height_le

/--
Alternative definition of height, with the supremum ranging only over those series that end at `a`.
-/
lemma height_eq_iSup_last_eq (a : α) :
    height a = ⨆ (p : LTSeries α) (_ : p.last = a), ↑(p.length) := by
  apply eq_of_forall_ge_iff
  intro n
  rw [height_le_iff', iSup₂_le_iff]

set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
/--
Alternative definition of coheight, with the supremum only ranging over those series
that begin at `a`.
-/
lemma coheight_eq_iSup_head_eq (a : α) :
    coheight a = ⨆ (p : LTSeries α) (_ : p.head = a), ↑(p.length) := by
  change height (α := αᵒᵈ) a = ⨆ (p : LTSeries α) (_ : p.head = a), ↑(p.length)
  rw [height_eq_iSup_last_eq]
  apply Equiv.iSup_congr ⟨RelSeries.reverse, RelSeries.reverse, fun _ ↦ RelSeries.reverse_reverse _,
    fun _ ↦ RelSeries.reverse_reverse _⟩
  simp

/--
Variant of `coheight_le_iff` ranging only over those series that begin exactly on `a`.
-/
lemma coheight_le_iff' {a : α} {n : ℕ∞} :
    coheight a ≤ n ↔ ∀ ⦃p : LTSeries α⦄, p.head = a → p.length ≤ n := by
  rw [coheight_eq_iSup_head_eq, iSup₂_le_iff]

lemma coheight_le {a : α} {n : ℕ∞} (h : ∀ (p : LTSeries α), p.head = a → p.length ≤ n) :
    coheight a ≤ n :=
  coheight_le_iff'.mpr h

lemma length_le_height {p : LTSeries α} {x : α} (hlast : p.last ≤ x) :
    p.length ≤ height x := by
  by_cases hlen0 : p.length ≠ 0
  · let p' := p.eraseLast.snoc x (by
      apply lt_of_lt_of_le
      · apply p.step ⟨p.length - 1, by lia⟩
      · convert hlast
        simp only [Fin.succ_mk, RelSeries.last, Fin.last]
        congr; lia)
    suffices p'.length ≤ height x by
      simp only [RelSeries.snoc_length, RelSeries.eraseLast_length, Nat.cast_add, ENat.coe_sub,
        Nat.cast_one, p'] at this
      convert this
      norm_cast
      lia
    refine le_iSup₂_of_le p' ?_ le_rfl
    simp [p']
  · simp_all

set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
lemma length_le_coheight {x : α} {p : LTSeries α} (hhead : x ≤ p.head) :
    p.length ≤ coheight x :=
  length_le_height (α := αᵒᵈ) (p := p.reverse) (by simpa)

/--
The height of the last element in a series is larger or equal to the length of the series.
-/
lemma length_le_height_last {p : LTSeries α} : p.length ≤ height p.last :=
  length_le_height le_rfl

/--
The coheight of the first element in a series is larger or equal to the length of the series.
-/
lemma length_le_coheight_head {p : LTSeries α} : p.length ≤ coheight p.head :=
  length_le_coheight le_rfl

/--
The height of an element in a series is larger or equal to its index in the series.
-/
lemma index_le_height (p : LTSeries α) (i : Fin (p.length + 1)) : i ≤ height (p i) :=
  length_le_height_last (p := p.take i)

/--
The coheight of an element in a series is larger or equal to its reverse index in the series.
-/
lemma rev_index_le_coheight (p : LTSeries α) (i : Fin (p.length + 1)) : i.rev ≤ coheight (p i) := by
  simpa using index_le_height (α := αᵒᵈ) p.reverse i.rev

/--
In a maximally long series, i.e one as long as the height of the last element, the height of each
element is its index in the series.
-/
lemma height_eq_index_of_length_eq_height_last {p : LTSeries α} (h : p.length = height p.last)
    (i : Fin (p.length + 1)) : height (p i) = i := by
  refine le_antisymm (height_le ?_) (index_le_height p i)
  intro p' hp'
  have hp'' := length_le_height_last (p := p'.smash (p.drop i) (by simpa))
  simp [← h] at hp''; clear h
  norm_cast at *
  lia

set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
/--
In a maximally long series, i.e one as long as the coheight of the first element, the coheight of
each element is its reverse index in the series.
-/
lemma coheight_eq_index_of_length_eq_head_coheight {p : LTSeries α} (h : p.length = coheight p.head)
    (i : Fin (p.length + 1)) : coheight (p i) = i.rev := by
  simpa using height_eq_index_of_length_eq_height_last (α := αᵒᵈ) (p := p.reverse) (by simpa) i.rev

@[gcongr]
lemma height_mono : Monotone (α := α) height :=
  fun _ _ hab ↦ biSup_mono (fun _ hla => hla.trans hab)

@[gcongr]
lemma coheight_anti : Antitone (α := α) coheight :=
  (height_mono (α := αᵒᵈ)).dual_left

private lemma height_add_const (a : α) (n : ℕ∞) :
    height a + n = ⨆ (p : LTSeries α) (_ : p.last = a), p.length + n := by
  have hne : Nonempty { p : LTSeries α // p.last = a } := ⟨RelSeries.singleton _ a, rfl⟩
  rw [height_eq_iSup_last_eq, iSup_subtype', iSup_subtype', ENat.iSup_add]

/- For elements of finite height, `height` is strictly monotone. -/
@[gcongr] lemma height_strictMono {x y : α} (hxy : x < y) (hfin : height x < ⊤) :
    height x < height y := by
  rw [← ENat.add_one_le_iff hfin.ne, height_add_const, iSup₂_le_iff]
  intro p hlast
  have := length_le_height_last (p := p.snoc y (by simp [*]))
  simpa using this

lemma height_add_one_le {a b : α} (hab : a < b) : height a + 1 ≤ height b := by
  cases hfin : height a with
  | top =>
    have : ⊤ ≤ height b := by
      rw [← hfin]
      gcongr
    simp [this]
  | coe n =>
    apply Order.add_one_le_of_lt
    rw [← hfin]
    gcongr
    simp [hfin]

/- For elements of finite height, `coheight` is strictly antitone. -/
@[gcongr] lemma coheight_strictAnti {x y : α} (hyx : y < x) (hfin : coheight x < ⊤) :
    coheight x < coheight y :=
  height_strictMono (α := αᵒᵈ) hyx hfin

lemma coheight_add_one_le {a b : α} (hab : b < a) : coheight a + 1 ≤ coheight b := by
  cases hfin : coheight a with
  | top =>
    have : ⊤ ≤ coheight b := by
      rw [← hfin]
      gcongr
    simp [this]
  | coe n =>
    apply Order.add_one_le_of_lt
    rw [← hfin]
    gcongr
    simp [hfin]

lemma height_le_height_apply_of_strictMono (f : α → β) (hf : StrictMono f) (x : α) :
    height x ≤ height (f x) := by
  simp only [height_eq_iSup_last_eq]
  apply iSup₂_le
  intro p hlast
  apply le_iSup₂_of_le (p.map f hf) (by simp [hlast]) (by simp)

lemma coheight_le_coheight_apply_of_strictMono (f : α → β) (hf : StrictMono f) (x : α) :
    coheight x ≤ coheight (f x) := by
  apply height_le_height_apply_of_strictMono (α := αᵒᵈ)
  exact fun _ _ h ↦ hf h

@[simp]
lemma height_orderIso (f : α ≃o β) (x : α) : height (f x) = height x := by
  apply le_antisymm
  · simpa using height_le_height_apply_of_strictMono _ f.symm.strictMono (f x)
  · exact height_le_height_apply_of_strictMono _ f.strictMono x

lemma coheight_orderIso (f : α ≃o β) (x : α) : coheight (f x) = coheight x :=
  height_orderIso (α := αᵒᵈ) f.dual x

private lemma exists_eq_iSup_of_iSup_eq_coe {α : Type*} [Nonempty α] {f : α → ℕ∞} {n : ℕ}
    (h : (⨆ x, f x) = n) : ∃ x, f x = n := by
  obtain ⟨x, hx⟩ := ENat.sSup_mem_of_nonempty_of_lt_top (h ▸ ENat.coe_lt_top _)
  use x
  simpa [hx] using h

/-- There exists a series ending in an element for any length up to the element’s height. -/
lemma exists_series_of_le_height (a : α) {n : ℕ} (h : n ≤ height a) :
    ∃ p : LTSeries α, p.last = a ∧ p.length = n := by
  have hne : Nonempty { p : LTSeries α // p.last = a } := ⟨RelSeries.singleton _ a, rfl⟩
  cases ha : height a with
  | top =>
    clear h
    rw [height_eq_iSup_last_eq, iSup_subtype', ENat.iSup_coe_eq_top, bddAbove_def] at ha
    contrapose! ha
    use n
    rintro m ⟨⟨p, rfl⟩, hp⟩
    simp only at hp
    by_contra! hnm
    apply ha (p.drop ⟨m-n, by lia⟩) (by simp) (by simp; lia)
  | coe m =>
    rw [ha, Nat.cast_le] at h
    rw [height_eq_iSup_last_eq, iSup_subtype'] at ha
    obtain ⟨⟨p, hlast⟩, hlen⟩ := exists_eq_iSup_of_iSup_eq_coe ha
    simp only [Nat.cast_inj] at hlen
    use p.drop ⟨m-n, by lia⟩
    constructor
    · simp [hlast]
    · simp [hlen]; lia

lemma exists_series_of_le_coheight (a : α) {n : ℕ} (h : n ≤ coheight a) :
    ∃ p : LTSeries α, p.head = a ∧ p.length = n := by
  obtain ⟨p, hp, hl⟩ := exists_series_of_le_height (α := αᵒᵈ) a h
  exact ⟨p.reverse, by simpa, by simpa⟩

/-- For an element of finite height there exists a series ending in that element of that height. -/
lemma exists_series_of_height_eq_coe (a : α) {n : ℕ} (h : height a = n) :
    ∃ p : LTSeries α, p.last = a ∧ p.length = n :=
  exists_series_of_le_height a (le_of_eq h.symm)

lemma exists_series_of_coheight_eq_coe (a : α) {n : ℕ} (h : coheight a = n) :
    ∃ p : LTSeries α, p.head = a ∧ p.length = n :=
  exists_series_of_le_coheight a (le_of_eq h.symm)

/-- Another characterization of height, based on the supremum of the heights of elements below. -/
lemma height_eq_iSup_lt_height (x : α) : height x = ⨆ y < x, height y + 1 := by
  apply le_antisymm
  · apply height_le
    intro p hp
    cases hlen : p.length with
    | zero => simp
    | succ n =>
      apply le_iSup_of_le p.eraseLast.last
      apply le_iSup_of_le (by rw [← hp]; exact p.eraseLast_last_rel_last (by lia))
      rw [height_add_const]
      apply le_iSup₂_of_le p.eraseLast (by rfl) (by simp [hlen])
  · apply iSup₂_le; intro y hyx
    rw [height_add_const]
    apply iSup₂_le; intro p hp
    apply le_iSup₂_of_le (p.snoc x (hp ▸ hyx)) (by simp) (by simp)

/--
Another characterization of coheight, based on the supremum of the coheights of elements above.
-/
lemma coheight_eq_iSup_gt_coheight (x : α) : coheight x = ⨆ y > x, coheight y + 1 :=
  height_eq_iSup_lt_height (α := αᵒᵈ) x

lemma height_le_coe_iff {x : α} {n : ℕ} : height x ≤ n ↔ ∀ y < x, height y < n := by
  conv_lhs => rw [height_eq_iSup_lt_height, iSup₂_le_iff]
  congr! 2 with y _
  cases height y
  · simp
  · norm_cast

lemma coheight_le_coe_iff {x : α} {n : ℕ} : coheight x ≤ n ↔ ∀ y > x, coheight y < n :=
  height_le_coe_iff (α := αᵒᵈ)

/--
The height of an element is infinite iff there exist series of arbitrary length ending in that
element.
-/
lemma height_eq_top_iff {x : α} :
    height x = ⊤ ↔ ∀ n, ∃ p : LTSeries α, p.last = x ∧ p.length = n where
  mp h n := by
    apply exists_series_of_le_height x (n := n)
    simp [h]
  mpr h := by
    rw [height_eq_iSup_last_eq, iSup_subtype', ENat.iSup_coe_eq_top, bddAbove_def]
    push Not
    intro n
    obtain ⟨p, hlast, hp⟩ := h (n + 1)
    exact ⟨p.length, ⟨⟨⟨p, hlast⟩, by simp [hp]⟩, by simp [hp]⟩⟩

set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
/--
The coheight of an element is infinite iff there exist series of arbitrary length ending in that
element.
-/
lemma coheight_eq_top_iff {x : α} :
    coheight x = ⊤ ↔ ∀ n, ∃ p : LTSeries α, p.head = x ∧ p.length = n := by
  convert height_eq_top_iff (α := αᵒᵈ) (x := x) using 2 with n
  constructor <;> (intro ⟨p, hp, hl⟩; use p.reverse; constructor <;> simpa)

/-- The elements of height zero are the minimal elements. -/
@[simp] lemma height_eq_zero {x : α} : height x = 0 ↔ IsMin x := by
  simpa [isMin_iff_forall_not_lt] using height_le_coe_iff (x := x) (n := 0)

protected alias ⟨_, IsMin.height_eq_zero⟩ := height_eq_zero

/-- The elements of coheight zero are the maximal elements. -/
@[simp] lemma coheight_eq_zero {x : α} : coheight x = 0 ↔ IsMax x :=
  height_eq_zero (α := αᵒᵈ)

protected alias ⟨_, IsMax.coheight_eq_zero⟩ := coheight_eq_zero

lemma height_ne_zero {x : α} : height x ≠ 0 ↔ ¬ IsMin x := height_eq_zero.not

@[simp] lemma height_pos {x : α} : 0 < height x ↔ ¬ IsMin x := by
  simp [pos_iff_ne_zero]

lemma coheight_ne_zero {x : α} : coheight x ≠ 0 ↔ ¬ IsMax x := coheight_eq_zero.not

@[simp] lemma coheight_pos {x : α} : 0 < coheight x ↔ ¬ IsMax x := by
  simp [pos_iff_ne_zero]

@[simp] lemma height_bot (α : Type*) [Preorder α] [OrderBot α] : height (⊥ : α) = 0 := by simp

@[simp] lemma coheight_top (α : Type*) [Preorder α] [OrderTop α] : coheight (⊤ : α) = 0 := by simp

lemma height_pos_of_bot_lt {x : α} [OrderBot α] (h : ⊥ < x) : 0 < height x := by
  rw [height_pos]
  grind [not_isMin_iff]

lemma coheight_pos_of_lt_top {x : α} [OrderTop α] (h : x < ⊤) : 0 < coheight x := by
  rw [coheight_pos]
  grind [not_isMax_iff]

lemma coe_lt_height_iff {x : α} {n : ℕ} (hfin : height x < ⊤) :
    n < height x ↔ ∃ y < x, height y = n where
  mp h := by
    obtain ⟨m, hx : height x = m⟩ := Option.ne_none_iff_exists'.mp hfin.ne_top
    rw [hx] at h; norm_cast at h
    obtain ⟨p, hp, hlen⟩ := exists_series_of_height_eq_coe x hx
    use p ⟨n, by lia⟩
    constructor
    · rw [← hp]
      apply LTSeries.strictMono
      simp [Fin.last]; lia
    · exact height_eq_index_of_length_eq_height_last (by simp [hlen, hp, hx]) ⟨n, by lia⟩
  mpr := fun ⟨y, hyx, hy⟩ =>
    hy ▸ height_strictMono hyx (lt_of_le_of_lt (height_mono hyx.le) hfin)

lemma coe_lt_coheight_iff {x : α} {n : ℕ} (hfin : coheight x < ⊤) :
    n < coheight x ↔ ∃ y > x, coheight y = n :=
  coe_lt_height_iff (α := αᵒᵈ) hfin

lemma height_eq_coe_add_one_iff {x : α} {n : ℕ} :
    height x = n + 1 ↔ height x < ⊤ ∧ (∃ y < x, height y = n) ∧ (∀ y < x, height y ≤ n) := by
  wlog hfin : height x < ⊤
  · simp_all [← Nat.cast_add_one, -Nat.cast_add]
  simp only [hfin, true_and]
  trans n < height x ∧ height x ≤ n + 1
  · rw [le_antisymm_iff, and_comm]
    simp [ENat.add_one_le_iff]
  · congr! 1
    · exact coe_lt_height_iff hfin
    · simpa [hfin, ENat.lt_add_one_iff] using height_le_coe_iff (x := x) (n := n + 1)

lemma coheight_eq_coe_add_one_iff {x : α} {n : ℕ} :
    coheight x = n + 1 ↔
      coheight x < ⊤ ∧ (∃ y > x, coheight y = n) ∧ (∀ y > x, coheight y ≤ n) :=
  height_eq_coe_add_one_iff (α := αᵒᵈ)

lemma height_eq_coe_iff {x : α} {n : ℕ} :
    height x = n ↔
      height x < ⊤ ∧ (n = 0 ∨ ∃ y < x, height y = n - 1) ∧ (∀ y < x, height y < n) := by
  wlog hfin : height x < ⊤
  · simp_all
  simp only [hfin, true_and]
  cases n
  case zero => simp [isMin_iff_forall_not_lt]
  case succ n =>
    simp only [Nat.cast_add, Nat.cast_one, add_eq_zero, one_ne_zero, and_false, false_or]
    rw [height_eq_coe_add_one_iff]
    simp only [hfin, true_and]
    congr! 3
    rename_i y _
    cases height y <;> simp; norm_cast; lia

lemma coheight_eq_coe_iff {x : α} {n : ℕ} :
    coheight x = n ↔
      coheight x < ⊤ ∧ (n = 0 ∨ ∃ y > x, coheight y = n - 1) ∧ (∀ y > x, coheight y < n) :=
  height_eq_coe_iff (α := αᵒᵈ)

/-- The elements of finite height `n` are the minimal elements among those of height `≥ n`. -/
lemma height_eq_coe_iff_minimal_le_height {a : α} {n : ℕ} :
    height a = n ↔ Minimal (fun y => n ≤ height y) a := by
  by_cases! hfin : height a < ⊤
  · cases hn : n with
    | zero => simp
    | succ => simp [minimal_iff_forall_lt, height_eq_coe_add_one_iff, ENat.add_one_le_iff,
        coe_lt_height_iff, *]
  · suffices ∃ x < a, ↑n ≤ height x by
      simp_all [minimal_iff_forall_lt]
    simp only [top_le_iff, height_eq_top_iff] at hfin
    obtain ⟨p, rfl, hp⟩ := hfin (n + 1)
    use p.eraseLast.last, p.eraseLast_last_rel_last (by lia)
    simpa [hp] using length_le_height_last (p := p.eraseLast)

/-- The elements of finite coheight `n` are the maximal elements among those of coheight `≥ n`. -/
lemma coheight_eq_coe_iff_maximal_le_coheight {a : α} {n : ℕ} :
    coheight a = n ↔ Maximal (fun y => n ≤ coheight y) a :=
  height_eq_coe_iff_minimal_le_height (α := αᵒᵈ)

lemma one_lt_height_iff {x : α} : 1 < Order.height x ↔ ∃ y z, z < y ∧ y < x := by
  rw [← ENat.add_one_le_iff ENat.one_ne_top, one_add_one_eq_two]
  refine ⟨fun h ↦ ?_, ?_⟩
  · obtain ⟨p, hp, hlen⟩ := Order.exists_series_of_le_height x (n := 2) h
    refine ⟨p 1, p 0, p.rel_of_lt ?_, hp ▸ p.rel_of_lt ?_⟩ <;> simp [Fin.lt_def, hlen]
  · rintro ⟨y, z, hzy, hyx⟩
    let p : LTSeries α := RelSeries.fromListIsChain [z, y, x] (List.cons_ne_nil z [y, x])
      (List.IsChain.cons_cons hzy <| List.isChain_pair.mpr hyx)
    have : p.last = x := by simp [p, ← RelSeries.getLast_toList]
    exact Order.length_le_height this.le

end height

/-!
## Krull dimension
-/

section krullDim

variable {α β : Type*}

variable [Preorder α] [Preorder β]

lemma LTSeries.length_le_krullDim (p : LTSeries α) : p.length ≤ krullDim α := le_sSup ⟨_, rfl⟩

@[simp]
lemma krullDim_eq_bot_iff : krullDim α = ⊥ ↔ IsEmpty α := by
  rw [eq_bot_iff, krullDim, iSup_le_iff]
  simp only [le_bot_iff, WithBot.natCast_ne_bot, isEmpty_iff]
  exact ⟨fun H x ↦ H ⟨0, fun _ ↦ x, by simp⟩, (· <| · 1)⟩

lemma krullDim_nonneg_iff : 0 ≤ krullDim α ↔ Nonempty α := by
  contrapose!
  rw [← krullDim_eq_bot_iff, ← WithBot.lt_coe_bot, bot_eq_zero, WithBot.coe_zero]

lemma krullDim_eq_bot [IsEmpty α] : krullDim α = ⊥ := krullDim_eq_bot_iff.mpr ‹_›

lemma krullDim_nonneg [Nonempty α] : 0 ≤ krullDim α := krullDim_nonneg_iff.mpr ‹_›

theorem krullDim_ne_bot_iff : krullDim α ≠ ⊥ ↔ Nonempty α := by
  rw [ne_eq, krullDim_eq_bot_iff, not_isEmpty_iff]

theorem bot_lt_krullDim_iff : ⊥ < krullDim α ↔ Nonempty α := by
  rw [bot_lt_iff_ne_bot, krullDim_ne_bot_iff]

theorem bot_lt_krullDim [Nonempty α] : ⊥ < krullDim α :=
  bot_lt_krullDim_iff.mpr ‹_›

lemma krullDim_nonpos_iff_forall_isMax : krullDim α ≤ 0 ↔ ∀ x : α, IsMax x := by
  simp only [krullDim, iSup_le_iff, isMax_iff_forall_not_lt]
  refine ⟨fun H x y h ↦ (H ⟨1, ![x, y],
    fun i ↦ by obtain rfl := Subsingleton.elim i 0; simpa⟩).not_gt (by simp), ?_⟩
  · rintro H ⟨_ | n, l, h⟩
    · simp
    · cases H (l 0) (l 1) (h 0)

lemma krullDim_nonpos_iff_forall_isMin : krullDim α ≤ 0 ↔ ∀ x : α, IsMin x := by
  simp only [krullDim_nonpos_iff_forall_isMax, IsMax, IsMin]
  exact forall_comm

lemma krullDim_le_one_iff : krullDim α ≤ 1 ↔ ∀ x : α, IsMin x ∨ IsMax x := by
  simp_rw [isMax_iff_forall_not_lt, isMin_iff_forall_not_lt, krullDim, iSup_le_iff]
  contrapose!
  constructor
  · rintro ⟨⟨_ | _ | n, l, hl⟩, hl'⟩
    iterate 2 · cases hl'.not_ge (by simp)
    exact ⟨l 1, ⟨l 0, hl 0⟩, l 2, hl 1⟩
  · rintro ⟨x, ⟨y, hxy⟩, z, hzx⟩
    exact ⟨⟨2, ![y, x, z], fun i ↦ by fin_cases i <;> simpa⟩, by simp⟩

lemma krullDim_pos_iff : 0 < krullDim α ↔ ∃ x y : α, x < y := by
  contrapose!
  simp_rw [← isMax_iff_forall_not_lt, ← krullDim_nonpos_iff_forall_isMax]

lemma one_le_krullDim_iff : 1 ≤ krullDim α ↔ ∃ x y : α, x < y := by
  rw [← krullDim_pos_iff, ← Nat.cast_zero, ← ENat.WithBot.add_one_le_iff, Nat.cast_zero, zero_add]

lemma krullDim_nonpos_of_subsingleton [Subsingleton α] : krullDim α ≤ 0 := by
  rw [krullDim_nonpos_iff_forall_isMax]
  exact fun x y h ↦ (Subsingleton.elim x y).ge

lemma krullDim_eq_zero [Nonempty α] [Subsingleton α] :
    krullDim α = 0 :=
  le_antisymm krullDim_nonpos_of_subsingleton krullDim_nonneg

lemma krullDim_eq_zero_of_unique [Unique α] : krullDim α = 0 :=
  le_antisymm krullDim_nonpos_of_subsingleton krullDim_nonneg

section PartialOrder

variable {α : Type*} [PartialOrder α]

lemma krullDim_le_one_iff_forall_isMax [OrderBot α] :
    krullDim α ≤ 1 ↔ ∀ x : α, x ≠ ⊥ → IsMax x := by
  simp [krullDim_le_one_iff, ← or_iff_not_imp_left]

lemma krullDim_eq_zero_iff_of_orderBot [OrderBot α] :
    krullDim α = 0 ↔ Subsingleton α :=
  ⟨fun H ↦ subsingleton_of_forall_eq ⊥ fun _ ↦ le_bot_iff.mp
    (krullDim_nonpos_iff_forall_isMax.mp H.le ⊥ bot_le), fun _ ↦ Order.krullDim_eq_zero⟩

lemma krullDim_pos_iff_of_orderBot [OrderBot α] :
    0 < krullDim α ↔ Nontrivial α := by
  rw [← not_subsingleton_iff_nontrivial, ← Order.krullDim_eq_zero_iff_of_orderBot,
    ← ne_eq, ← lt_or_lt_iff_ne, or_iff_right]
  simp [Order.krullDim_nonneg]

lemma krullDim_le_one_iff_forall_isMin [OrderTop α] :
    krullDim α ≤ 1 ↔ ∀ x : α, x ≠ ⊤ → IsMin x := by
  simp [krullDim_le_one_iff, ← or_iff_not_imp_right]

lemma krullDim_eq_zero_iff_of_orderTop [OrderTop α] :
    krullDim α = 0 ↔ Subsingleton α :=
  ⟨fun H ↦ subsingleton_of_forall_eq ⊤ fun _ ↦ top_le_iff.mp
    (krullDim_nonpos_iff_forall_isMin.mp H.le ⊤ le_top), fun _ ↦ Order.krullDim_eq_zero⟩

lemma krullDim_pos_iff_of_orderTop [OrderTop α] :
    0 < krullDim α ↔ Nontrivial α := by
  rw [← not_subsingleton_iff_nontrivial, ← Order.krullDim_eq_zero_iff_of_orderTop,
    ← ne_eq, ← lt_or_lt_iff_ne, or_iff_right]
  simp [Order.krullDim_nonneg]

lemma krullDim_le_one_iff_of_boundedOrder [BoundedOrder α] :
    krullDim α ≤ 1 ↔ ∀ x : α, x = ⊥ ∨ x = ⊤ := by
  simp [Order.krullDim_le_one_iff]

end PartialOrder

lemma krullDim_eq_length_of_finiteDimensionalOrder [FiniteDimensionalOrder α] :
    krullDim α = (LTSeries.longestOf α).length :=
  le_antisymm
    (iSup_le <| fun _ ↦ WithBot.coe_le_coe.mpr <| WithTop.coe_le_coe.mpr <|
      RelSeries.length_le_length_longestOf _ _) <|
    le_iSup (fun (i : LTSeries _) ↦ (i.length : WithBot (WithTop ℕ))) <| LTSeries.longestOf _

set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false in
lemma krullDim_eq_top [InfiniteDimensionalOrder α] :
    krullDim α = ⊤ :=
  le_antisymm le_top <| le_iSup_iff.mpr <| fun m hm ↦ match m, hm with
  | ⊥, hm => False.elim <| by
    haveI : Inhabited α := ⟨LTSeries.withLength _ 0 0⟩
    exact not_le_of_gt (WithBot.bot_lt_coe _ : ⊥ < (0 : WithBot (WithTop ℕ))) <| hm default
  | some ⊤, _ => le_refl _
  | some (some m), hm => by
    refine (not_lt_of_ge (hm (LTSeries.withLength _ (m + 1))) ?_).elim
    rw [WithBot.some_eq_coe, ← WithBot.coe_natCast, WithBot.coe_lt_coe,
      WithTop.some_eq_coe, ← WithTop.coe_natCast, WithTop.coe_lt_coe]
    simp

lemma krullDim_eq_top_iff : krullDim α = ⊤ ↔ InfiniteDimensionalOrder α := by
  refine ⟨fun h ↦ ?_, fun _ ↦ krullDim_eq_top⟩
  cases isEmpty_or_nonempty α
  · simp [krullDim_eq_bot] at h
  cases finiteDimensionalOrder_or_infiniteDimensionalOrder α
  · rw [krullDim_eq_length_of_finiteDimensionalOrder] at h
    cases h
  · infer_instance

lemma le_krullDim_iff {n : ℕ} : n ≤ krullDim α ↔ ∃ l : LTSeries α, l.length = n := by
  cases isEmpty_or_nonempty α
  · simp [krullDim_eq_bot]
  cases finiteDimensionalOrder_or_infiniteDimensionalOrder α
  · rw [krullDim_eq_length_of_finiteDimensionalOrder, Nat.cast_le]
    constructor
    · exact fun H ↦ ⟨(LTSeries.longestOf α).take ⟨_, Nat.lt_succ_of_le H⟩, rfl⟩
    · exact fun ⟨l, hl⟩ ↦ hl ▸ l.longestOf_is_longest
  · simpa [krullDim_eq_top] using SetRel.InfiniteDimensional.exists_relSeries_with_length n

/-- A definition of krullDim for nonempty `α` that avoids `WithBot` -/
lemma krullDim_eq_iSup_length [Nonempty α] :
    krullDim α = ⨆ (p : LTSeries α), (p.length : ℕ∞) := by
  simp [krullDim, WithBot.coe_iSup (OrderTop.bddAbove _), WithBot.coe_natCast]

lemma krullDim_lt_coe_iff {n : ℕ} : krullDim α < n ↔ ∀ l : LTSeries α, l.length < n := by
  rw [krullDim, ← WithBot.coe_natCast]
  rcases n with - | n
  · rw [ENat.coe_zero, ← bot_eq_zero, WithBot.lt_coe_bot]
    simp
  · simp [ENat.WithBot.lt_add_one_iff, WithBot.coe_natCast]

lemma krullDim_le_of_strictMono (f : α → β) (hf : StrictMono f) : krullDim α ≤ krullDim β :=
  iSup_le fun p ↦ le_sSup ⟨p.map f hf, rfl⟩

lemma krullDim_le_of_strictComono_and_surj
    (f : α → β) (hf : ∀ ⦃a b⦄, f a < f b → a < b) (hf' : Function.Surjective f) :
    krullDim β ≤ krullDim α :=
  iSup_le fun p ↦ le_sSup ⟨p.comap _ hf hf', rfl⟩

lemma krullDim_eq_of_orderIso (f : α ≃o β) : krullDim α = krullDim β :=
  le_antisymm (krullDim_le_of_strictMono _ f.strictMono) <|
    krullDim_le_of_strictMono _ f.symm.strictMono

@[simp] lemma krullDim_orderDual : krullDim αᵒᵈ = krullDim α :=
  le_antisymm (iSup_le fun i ↦ le_sSup ⟨i.reverse, rfl⟩) <|
    iSup_le fun i ↦ le_sSup ⟨i.reverse, rfl⟩

lemma height_le_krullDim (a : α) : height a ≤ krullDim α := by
  have : Nonempty α := ⟨a⟩
  rw [krullDim_eq_iSup_length]
  simp only [WithBot.coe_le_coe]
  exact height_le fun p _ ↦ le_iSup_of_le p le_rfl

lemma coheight_le_krullDim (a : α) : coheight a ≤ krullDim α := by
  simpa using height_le_krullDim (α := αᵒᵈ) a

@[simp]
lemma _root_.LTSeries.height_last_longestOf [FiniteDimensionalOrder α] :
    height (LTSeries.longestOf α).last = krullDim α := by
  refine le_antisymm (height_le_krullDim _) ?_
  rw [krullDim_eq_length_of_finiteDimensionalOrder, height]
  norm_cast
  exact le_iSup_iff.mpr <| fun _ h ↦ iSup_le_iff.mp (h _) le_rfl

/--
The Krull dimension is the supremum of the elements' heights.

This version of the lemma assumes that `α` is nonempty. In this case, the coercion from `ℕ∞` to
`WithBot ℕ∞` is on the outside of the right-hand side, which is usually more convenient.

If `α` were empty, then `krullDim α = ⊥`. See `krullDim_eq_iSup_height` for the more general
version, with the coercion under the supremum.
-/
lemma krullDim_eq_iSup_height_of_nonempty [Nonempty α] : krullDim α = ↑(⨆ (a : α), height a) := by
  apply le_antisymm
  · apply iSup_le
    intro p
    suffices p.length ≤ ⨆ (a : α), height a from (WithBot.unbotD_le_iff fun _ => this).mp this
    apply le_iSup_of_le p.last (length_le_height_last (p := p))
  · rw [WithBot.coe_iSup (by bddDefault)]
    apply iSup_le
    apply height_le_krullDim

/--
The Krull dimension is the supremum of the elements' coheights.

This version of the lemma assumes that `α` is nonempty. In this case, the coercion from `ℕ∞` to
`WithBot ℕ∞` is on the outside of the right-hand side, which is usually more convenient.

If `α` were empty, then `krullDim α = ⊥`. See `krullDim_eq_iSup_coheight` for the more general
version, with the coercion under the supremum.
-/
lemma krullDim_eq_iSup_coheight_of_nonempty [Nonempty α] :
    krullDim α = ↑(⨆ (a : α), coheight a) := by
  simpa using krullDim_eq_iSup_height_of_nonempty (α := αᵒᵈ)

/--
The Krull dimension is the supremum of the elements' height plus coheight.
-/
lemma krullDim_eq_iSup_height_add_coheight_of_nonempty [Nonempty α] :
    krullDim α = ↑(⨆ (a : α), height a + coheight a) := by
  apply le_antisymm
  · rw [krullDim_eq_iSup_height_of_nonempty, WithBot.coe_le_coe]
    apply ciSup_mono (by bddDefault) (by simp)
  · wlog hnottop : krullDim α < ⊤
    · simp_all
    rw [krullDim_eq_iSup_length, WithBot.coe_le_coe]
    apply iSup_le
    intro a
    have : height a < ⊤ := WithBot.coe_lt_coe.mp (lt_of_le_of_lt (height_le_krullDim a) hnottop)
    have : coheight a < ⊤ := WithBot.coe_lt_coe.mp (lt_of_le_of_lt (coheight_le_krullDim a) hnottop)
    cases hh : height a with
    | top => simp_all
    | coe n =>
      cases hch : coheight a with
      | top => simp_all
      | coe m =>
        obtain ⟨p₁, hlast, hlen₁⟩ := exists_series_of_height_eq_coe a hh
        obtain ⟨p₂, hhead, hlen₂⟩ := exists_series_of_coheight_eq_coe a hch
        apply le_iSup_of_le ((p₁.smash p₂) (by simp [*])) (by simp [*])

/--
The Krull dimension is the supremum of the elements' heights.

If `α` is `Nonempty`, then `krullDim_eq_iSup_height_of_nonempty`, with the coercion from
`ℕ∞` to `WithBot ℕ∞` outside the supremum, can be more convenient.
-/
lemma krullDim_eq_iSup_height : krullDim α = ⨆ (a : α), ↑(height a) := by
  cases isEmpty_or_nonempty α with
  | inl h => rw [krullDim_eq_bot, ciSup_of_empty]
  | inr h => rw [krullDim_eq_iSup_height_of_nonempty, WithBot.coe_iSup (OrderTop.bddAbove _)]

/--
The Krull dimension is the supremum of the elements' coheights.

If `α` is `Nonempty`, then `krullDim_eq_iSup_coheight_of_nonempty`, with the coercion from
`ℕ∞` to `WithBot ℕ∞` outside the supremum, can be more convenient.
-/
lemma krullDim_eq_iSup_coheight : krullDim α = ⨆ (a : α), ↑(coheight a) := by
  cases isEmpty_or_nonempty α with
  | inl h => rw [krullDim_eq_bot, ciSup_of_empty]
  | inr h => rw [krullDim_eq_iSup_coheight_of_nonempty, WithBot.coe_iSup (OrderTop.bddAbove _)]

@[simp] -- not as useful as a simp lemma as it looks, due to the coe on the left
lemma height_top_eq_krullDim [OrderTop α] : height (⊤ : α) = krullDim α := by
  rw [krullDim_eq_iSup_length]
  simp only [WithBot.coe_inj]
  apply le_antisymm
  · exact height_le fun p _ ↦ le_iSup_of_le p le_rfl
  · exact iSup_le fun _ => length_le_height le_top

@[simp] -- not as useful as a simp lemma as it looks, due to the coe on the left
lemma coheight_bot_eq_krullDim [OrderBot α] : coheight (⊥ : α) = krullDim α := by
  rw [← krullDim_orderDual]
  exact height_top_eq_krullDim (α := αᵒᵈ)

lemma height_eq_krullDim_Iic (x : α) : (height x : ℕ∞) = krullDim (Set.Iic x) := by
  rw [← height_top_eq_krullDim, height, height, WithBot.coe_inj]
  apply le_antisymm
  · apply iSup_le; intro p; apply iSup_le; intro hp
    let q := LTSeries.mk p.length (fun i ↦ (⟨p.toFun i, le_trans (p.monotone (Fin.le_last _)) hp⟩
     : Set.Iic x)) (fun _ _ h ↦ p.strictMono h)
    simp only [le_top, iSup_pos, ge_iff_le]
    exact le_iSup (fun p ↦ (p.length : ℕ∞)) q
  · apply iSup_le; intro p; apply iSup_le; intro _
    have mono : StrictMono (fun (y : Set.Iic x) ↦ y.1) := fun _ _ h ↦ h
    rw [← LTSeries.map_length p (fun x ↦ x.1) mono, ]
    refine le_iSup₂ (f := fun p hp ↦ (p.length : ℕ∞)) (p.map (fun x ↦ x.1) mono) ?_
    exact (p.toFun (Fin.last p.length)).2

lemma coheight_eq_krullDim_Ici {α : Type*} [Preorder α] (x : α) :
    (coheight x : ℕ∞) = krullDim (Set.Ici x) := by
  rw [coheight, ← krullDim_orderDual, Order.krullDim_eq_of_orderIso (OrderIso.refl _)]
  exact height_eq_krullDim_Iic _

end krullDim

section finiteDimensional

variable {α : Type*} [Preorder α]

lemma finiteDimensionalOrder_iff_krullDim_ne_bot_and_top :
    FiniteDimensionalOrder α ↔ krullDim α ≠ ⊥ ∧ krullDim α ≠ ⊤ := by
  by_cases h : Nonempty α
  · simp [← not_infiniteDimensionalOrder_iff, ← krullDim_eq_top_iff]
  · constructor
    · exact (fun h1 ↦ False.elim (h (LTSeries.nonempty_of_finiteDimensionalOrder α)))
    · exact (fun h1 ↦ False.elim (h1.1 (krullDim_eq_bot_iff.mpr (not_nonempty_iff.mp h))))

lemma krullDim_ne_bot_of_finiteDimensionalOrder [FiniteDimensionalOrder α] : krullDim α ≠ ⊥ :=
  (finiteDimensionalOrder_iff_krullDim_ne_bot_and_top.mp ‹_›).1

lemma krullDim_ne_top_of_finiteDimensionalOrder [FiniteDimensionalOrder α] : krullDim α ≠ ⊤ :=
  (finiteDimensionalOrder_iff_krullDim_ne_bot_and_top.mp ‹_›).2

lemma coheight_lt_top [FiniteDimensionalOrder α] (x : α) : coheight x < ⊤ := by
  rw [← WithBot.coe_lt_coe]
  apply lt_of_le_of_lt (coheight_le_krullDim x)
  simpa using krullDim_ne_top_of_finiteDimensionalOrder.lt_top

end finiteDimensional

section typeclass

/-- Typeclass for orders with krull dimension at most `n`. -/
@[mk_iff]
class KrullDimLE (n : ℕ) (α : Type*) [Preorder α] : Prop where
  krullDim_le : krullDim α ≤ n

lemma KrullDimLE.mono {n m : ℕ} (e : n ≤ m) (α : Type*) [Preorder α] [KrullDimLE n α] :
    KrullDimLE m α :=
  ⟨KrullDimLE.krullDim_le (n := n).trans (Nat.cast_le.mpr e)⟩

instance {α} [Preorder α] [Subsingleton α] : KrullDimLE 0 α := ⟨krullDim_nonpos_of_subsingleton⟩

end typeclass
