## Concrete calculations
-/

section calculations

lemma krullDim_eq_one_iff_of_boundedOrder {α : Type*} [PartialOrder α] [BoundedOrder α] :
    krullDim α = 1 ↔ IsSimpleOrder α := by
  rw [le_antisymm_iff, krullDim_le_one_iff_of_boundedOrder, WithBot.one_le_iff_pos,
    Order.krullDim_pos_iff_of_orderBot, isSimpleOrder_iff, and_comm]

@[simp] lemma krullDim_of_isSimpleOrder {α : Type*} [PartialOrder α] [BoundedOrder α]
    [IsSimpleOrder α] : krullDim α = 1 :=
  krullDim_eq_one_iff_of_boundedOrder.mpr ‹_›

variable {α : Type*} [Preorder α]

/-
These two lemmas could possibly be used to simplify the subsequent calculations,
especially once the `Set.encard` api is richer.

(Commented out to avoid importing modules purely for `proof_wanted`.)
proof_wanted height_of_linearOrder {α : Type*} [LinearOrder α] (a : α) :
  height a = (Set.Iio a).encard

proof_wanted coheight_of_linearOrder {α : Type*} [LinearOrder α] (a : α) :
  coheight a = (Set.Ioi a).encard
-/

@[simp] lemma height_nat (n : ℕ) : height n = n := by
  induction n using Nat.strongRecOn with | ind n ih =>
  apply le_antisymm
  · apply height_le_coe_iff.mpr
    simp +contextual only [ih, Nat.cast_lt, implies_true]
  · exact length_le_height_last (p := LTSeries.range n)

@[simp] lemma coheight_of_noMaxOrder [NoMaxOrder α] (a : α) : coheight a = ⊤ := by
  obtain ⟨f, hstrictmono⟩ := Nat.exists_strictMono ↑(Set.Ioi a)
  apply coheight_eq_top_iff.mpr
  intro m
  use { length := m, toFun := fun i => if i = 0 then a else f i, step := ?step }
  case h => simp [RelSeries.head]
  case step =>
    intro ⟨i, hi⟩
    by_cases hzero : i = 0
    · subst i
      exact (f 1).prop
    · suffices f i < f (i + 1) by simp [Fin.ext_iff, hzero, this]
      apply hstrictmono
      lia

@[simp] lemma height_of_noMinOrder [NoMinOrder α] (a : α) : height a = ⊤ :=
  -- Implementation note: Here it's a bit easier to define the coheight variant first
  coheight_of_noMaxOrder (α := αᵒᵈ) a

@[simp] lemma krullDim_of_noMaxOrder [Nonempty α] [NoMaxOrder α] : krullDim α = ⊤ := by
  simp [krullDim_eq_iSup_coheight, coheight_of_noMaxOrder]

@[simp] lemma krullDim_of_noMinOrder [Nonempty α] [NoMinOrder α] : krullDim α = ⊤ := by
  simp [krullDim_eq_iSup_height, height_of_noMinOrder]

lemma coheight_nat (n : ℕ) : coheight n = ⊤ := coheight_of_noMaxOrder ..

lemma krullDim_nat : krullDim ℕ = ⊤ := krullDim_of_noMaxOrder ..

lemma height_int (n : ℤ) : height n = ⊤ := height_of_noMinOrder ..

lemma coheight_int (n : ℤ) : coheight n = ⊤ := coheight_of_noMaxOrder ..

lemma krullDim_int : krullDim ℤ = ⊤ := krullDim_of_noMaxOrder ..

@[simp] lemma height_coe_withBot (x : α) : height (x : WithBot α) = height x + 1 := by
  apply le_antisymm
  · apply height_le
    intro p hlast
    wlog hlenpos : p.length ≠ 0
    · simp_all
    -- essentially p' := (p.drop 1).map unbot
    let p' : LTSeries α := {
      length := p.length - 1
      toFun := fun ⟨i, hi⟩ => (p ⟨i+1, by lia⟩).unbot (by
        apply ne_bot_of_gt (b := p.head)
        apply p.strictMono
        exact compare_gt_iff_gt.mp rfl)
      step := fun i => by simpa [WithBot.unbot_lt_iff] using p.step ⟨i + 1, by lia⟩ }
    have hlast' : p'.last = x := by
      simp only [p', RelSeries.last, WithBot.unbot_eq_iff, ← hlast, Fin.last]
      congr
      lia
    suffices p'.length ≤ height p'.last by
      simpa [p', hlast'] using this
    apply length_le_height_last
  · rw [height_add_const]
    apply iSup₂_le
    intro p hlast
    let p' := (p.map _ WithBot.coe_strictMono).cons ⊥ (by simp)
    apply le_iSup₂_of_le p' (by simp [p', hlast]) (by simp [p'])

@[simp] lemma coheight_coe_withTop (x : α) : coheight (x : WithTop α) = coheight x + 1 :=
  height_coe_withBot (α := αᵒᵈ) x

@[simp] lemma height_coe_withTop (x : α) : height (x : WithTop α) = height x := by
  apply le_antisymm
  · apply height_le
    intro p hlast
    -- essentially p' := p.map untop
    let p' : LTSeries α := {
      length := p.length
      toFun := fun i => (p i).untop (by
        apply WithTop.lt_top_iff_ne_top.mp
        apply lt_of_le_of_lt
        · exact p.monotone (Fin.le_last _)
        · rw [RelSeries.last] at hlast
          simp [hlast])
      step := fun i => by simpa [WithTop.untop_lt_iff, WithTop.coe_untop] using p.step i }
    have hlast' : p'.last = x := by
      simp only [p', RelSeries.last, WithTop.untop_eq_iff, ← hlast]
    suffices p'.length ≤ height p'.last by
      rw [hlast'] at this
      simpa [p'] using this
    apply length_le_height_last
  · apply height_le
    intro p hlast
    let p' := p.map _ WithTop.coe_strictMono
    apply le_iSup₂_of_le p' (by simp [p', hlast]) (by simp [p'])

@[simp] lemma coheight_coe_withBot (x : α) : coheight (x : WithBot α) = coheight x :=
  height_coe_withTop (α := αᵒᵈ) x

@[simp] lemma krullDim_WithTop [Nonempty α] : krullDim (WithTop α) = krullDim α + 1 := by
  rw [← height_top_eq_krullDim, krullDim_eq_iSup_height_of_nonempty, height_eq_iSup_lt_height]
  norm_cast
  simp_rw [WithTop.lt_top_iff_ne_top]
  rw [ENat.iSup_add, iSup_subtype']
  symm
  apply Equiv.withTopSubtypeNe.symm.iSup_congr
  simp

@[simp] lemma krullDim_withBot [Nonempty α] : krullDim (WithBot α) = krullDim α + 1 := by
  conv_lhs => rw [← krullDim_orderDual]
  conv_rhs => rw [← krullDim_orderDual]
  exact krullDim_WithTop (α := αᵒᵈ)

@[simp]
lemma krullDim_enat : krullDim ℕ∞ = ⊤ := by
  change (krullDim (WithTop ℕ) = ⊤)
  simp [← WithBot.coe_top, ← WithBot.coe_one, ← WithBot.coe_add]

@[simp]
lemma height_enat (n : ℕ∞) : height n = n := by
  cases n with
  | top => simp only [← WithBot.coe_eq_coe, height_top_eq_krullDim, krullDim_enat, WithBot.coe_top]
  | coe n => exact (height_coe_withTop _).trans (height_nat _)

@[simp]
lemma coheight_coe_enat (n : ℕ) : coheight (n : ℕ∞) = ⊤ := by
  apply (coheight_coe_withTop _).trans
  simp only [coheight_nat, top_add]

end calculations

section orderHom

variable {α β : Type*} [Preorder α] [PartialOrder β]
variable {m : ℕ} (f : α →o β) (h : ∀ (x : β), Order.krullDim (f ⁻¹' {x}) ≤ m)

include h in
lemma height_le_of_krullDim_preimage_le (x : α) :
    Order.height x ≤ (m + 1) * Order.height (f x) + m := by
  generalize h' : Order.height (f x) = n
  cases n with | top => simp | coe n =>
    induction n using Nat.strong_induction_on generalizing x with | h n ih =>
    refine height_le_iff.mpr fun p hp ↦ le_of_not_gt fun h_len ↦ ?_
    let i : Fin (p.length + 1) := ⟨p.length - (m + 1), Nat.sub_lt_succ p.length _⟩
    suffices h'' : f (p i) < f x by
      obtain ⟨n', hn'⟩ : ∃ (n' : ℕ), n' = height (f (p i)) := ENat.ne_top_iff_exists.mp
        ((height_mono h''.le).trans_lt (h' ▸ ENat.coe_lt_top _)).ne
      have h_lt : n' < n := ENat.coe_lt_coe.mp
        (h' ▸ hn' ▸ height_strictMono h'' (hn' ▸ ENat.coe_lt_top _))
      have := (length_le_height_last (p := p.take i)).trans <| ih n' h_lt (p i) hn'.symm
      rw [RelSeries.take_length, ENat.coe_sub, Nat.cast_add, Nat.cast_one, tsub_le_iff_right,
        add_assoc, add_comm _ (_ + 1), ← add_assoc, ← mul_add_one] at this
      refine not_lt_of_ge ?_ (h_len.trans_le this)
      gcongr
      rwa [← ENat.coe_one, ← ENat.coe_add, ENat.coe_le_coe]
    refine (f.monotone ((p.monotone (Fin.le_last _)).trans hp)).lt_of_not_ge fun h'' ↦ ?_
    let q' : LTSeries α := p.drop i
    let q : LTSeries (f ⁻¹' {f x}) := ⟨q'.length, fun j ↦ ⟨q' j, le_antisymm
      (f.monotone (le_trans (b := q'.last) (q'.monotone (Fin.le_last _)) (p.last_drop _ ▸ hp)))
      (le_trans (b := f q'.head) (p.head_drop _ ▸ h'')
        (f.monotone (q'.monotone (Fin.zero_le _))))⟩, fun i ↦ q'.step i⟩
    have := (LTSeries.length_le_krullDim q).trans (h (f x))
    simp only [RelSeries.drop_length, Nat.cast_le, tsub_le_iff_right, q', i, q] at this
    have : p.length > m := ENat.coe_lt_coe.mp ((le_add_left le_rfl).trans_lt h_len)
    lia

include h in
lemma coheight_le_of_krullDim_preimage_le (x : α) :
    Order.coheight x ≤ (m + 1) * Order.coheight (f x) + m := by
  rw [Order.coheight, Order.coheight]
  apply height_le_of_krullDim_preimage_le (f := f.dual)
  exact fun x ↦ le_of_eq_of_le (krullDim_orderDual (α := f ⁻¹' {x})) (h x)

include f h in
lemma krullDim_le_of_krullDim_preimage_le :
    Order.krullDim α ≤ (m + 1) * Order.krullDim β + m := by
  rw [Order.krullDim_eq_iSup_height, Order.krullDim_eq_iSup_height, iSup_le_iff]
  refine fun x ↦ (WithBot.coe_mono (height_le_of_krullDim_preimage_le f h x)).trans ?_
  push_cast
  gcongr
  exacts [right_eq_inf.mp rfl, le_iSup_iff.mpr fun b a ↦ a (f x)]

/-- Another version when the `OrderHom` is unbundled -/
lemma krullDim_le_of_krullDim_preimage_le' (f : α → β) (h_mono : Monotone f)
    (h : ∀ (x : β), Order.krullDim (f ⁻¹' {x}) ≤ m) :
    Order.krullDim α ≤ (m + 1) * Order.krullDim β + m :=
  Order.krullDim_le_of_krullDim_preimage_le ⟨f, h_mono⟩ h

lemma krullDim_le_of_orderEmbedding (e : α ↪o β) : Order.krullDim α ≤ Order.krullDim β := by
  have (b : β) : Subsingleton (e ⁻¹' {b}) := Set.Subsingleton.coe_sort <|
    Set.Subsingleton.preimage Set.subsingleton_singleton e.injective
  simpa using Order.krullDim_le_of_krullDim_preimage_le' e e.monotone fun _ ↦
    Order.krullDim_nonpos_of_subsingleton

end orderHom

end Order
"}

import Mathlib
open AlgebraicGeometry

-- Sanity-check: for an integral scheme X with generic point η,
-- we want η to be MAXIMAL in the preorder (so coheight η = 0).
example (X : Scheme) [IrreducibleSpace X] :
    IsMax (genericPoint X) := by
  -- genericPoint is in closure{?} for all points -- so all points specialize to genericPoint
  -- x ≤ genericPoint iff genericPoint ⤳ x iff x ∈ closure{genericPoint} = X. Always true.
  intro y hy
  -- hy : genericPoint X ≤ y means y ⤳ genericPoint X
  -- i.e., genericPoint X ∈ closure{y}. Since closure{y} ⊂ X, we need
  -- genericPoint X is in closure{y}. This means y is a generization of genericPoint.
  -- For an irreducible space, the genericPoint is the unique point whose closure is X.
  -- If genericPoint ∈ closure{y}, then closure{y} ⊇ closure{genericPoint} = X, so y = genericPoint.
  -- So y ≤ genericPoint trivially.
  show y ≤ genericPoint X
  -- y ≤ genericPoint ↔ genericPoint ⤳ y ↔ y ∈ closure{genericPoint X} = X. Always true.
  exact (genericPoint_specializes y).symm |>.le

import Mathlib
open AlgebraicGeometry

-- Sanity-check the chain direction
example (X : Scheme) [IrreducibleSpace X] (y : X) : y ≤ genericPoint X := by
  show y ≤ genericPoint X
  -- y ≤ genericPoint iff genericPoint ⤳ y iff y ∈ closure {genericPoint} = X.
  -- always true.
  rw [show (y ≤ genericPoint X) ↔ (genericPoint X ⤳ y) from Iff.rfl]
  exact genericPoint_specializes y

\paragraph{Standing hypothesis $(*)$ in the Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).}
Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) does not bundle Hartshorne's four-clause
$(\ast)$ as a single typeclass. The chapter pins $(*)$ to a \emph{layered}
typeclass set that the prover-agent threads through the Lean signatures in
\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean}:

\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Basic formal-sum layer} (carrier of \Cref{def:prime_divisor} and
    \Cref{def:codim1_cycles}, the additive group $\Div(X)$, the degree
    \Cref{def:divisor_degree}, the degree-homomorphism
    \Cref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}, and \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}): the
    only Mathlib typeclass invoked is \texttt{[IsIntegral X]}
    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme} via
    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Properties}). Integrality of $X$ already
    delivers \texttt{IrreducibleSpace X.carrier} (the carrier-level instance
    chain), so $X$ has a unique generic point and the function field
    \texttt{X.functionField} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField})
    is a field; separatedness and noetherianness are \emph{not} required for
    the bare formal-sum data type.
  \item \textbf{Order/principal layer} (\Cref{def:order_at_point},
    \Cref{def:principal_divisor}, \Cref{thm:principal_hom}): the Lean
    signature additionally threads
    \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]}
    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Noetherian}) and Hartshorne's
    regular-in-codimension-one clause. The latter has \emph{no} Mathlib
    typeclass shipping it directly; the chapter's pin is the project-side
    predicate ``for every $x \in X$ with $\codim_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1$ the
    stalk $\mathcal O_{X,x}$ is a discrete valuation ring'', encodable as the
    conjunction $\forall x,\;\Order.coheight x = 1 \Rightarrow
    \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing}\,(\mathcal O_{X,x})$. Iter-173+ may
    introduce a \texttt{Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne} predicate to
    abbreviate this; until then the order/principal lemmas thread the DVR
    hypothesis explicitly at each codim-1 point.
  \item \textbf{Curve layer} (\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}): the standing
    hypothesis tightens to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
    over $\bar k$ with explicit integrality witness, namely
    \begin{quote}
      \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\

  \label{def:codim1_cycles}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor}
  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "prime divisor" and

  % conjunction. \emph{Consequence:} until that refinement lands, \texttt{Scheme.WeilDivisor X} is
  % structurally the free abelian group on \emph{all} points of $X$ rather than on codim-1
  % integral subschemes; downstream RR-side consumers must wait. (Flagged by iter-172
  % lean-auditor as must-fix-before-any-sibling-RR-file-consumes-PrimeDivisor.)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$. A \emph{prime
  % divisor} on $X$ is a closed integral subscheme $Y$ of codimension one.
  % A \emph{Weil divisor} is an element of the free abelian group
  % $\operatorname{Div} X$ generated by the prime divisors. We write a
  % divisor as $D = \sum n_i Y_i$, where the $Y_i$ are prime divisors, the
  % $n_i$ are integers, and only finitely many $n_i$ are different from
  % zero. If all the $n_i \geq 0$, we say that $D$ is \emph{effective}."
  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130.}
  Let $X$ be a Noetherian integral separated scheme that is regular in
  codimension one (i.e.\ $X$ satisfies $(*)$). A \emph{prime divisor} on
  $X$ is a closed integral subscheme $Y \subset X$ of codimension one. The
  \emph{Weil divisor group}
  \[
    \Div(X) \;=\; \bigoplus_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
    \mathbb Z \cdot Y
  \]
  is the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of $X$; an element
  $D = \sum n_i Y_i$ with finitely many nonzero coefficients $n_i \in
  \mathbb Z$ is called a \emph{Weil divisor}, and is \emph{effective} when
  all $n_i \geq 0$.
\end{definition}

\section{Codim-$1$ cycle group}

\begin{definition}

[Prime divisor of a scheme]
  \label{def:prime_divisor}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor}
  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "prime divisor" under
  % condition $(*)$) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (divisors).
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$. A \emph{prime
  % divisor} on $X$ is a closed integral subscheme $Y$ of codimension one."
  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of prime divisor).}
  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. A \emph{prime divisor} on $X$ is a closed integral
  subscheme $Y \subset X$ of codimension one. Equivalently, by the standard
  generic-point correspondence (Hartshorne II \S6, p.~130, ``let $\eta \in
  Y$ be its generic point\ldots''), $Y$ is determined by its generic point
  $\eta \in X$: closed integral subschemes of codimension one of $X$ are in
  natural bijection with points $x \in X$ such that the closure
  $\overline{\{x\}} \subset X$ has codimension one. The closure
  $\overline{\{x\}}$, equipped with the \emph{reduced induced subscheme
  structure}, is automatically integral (irreducible, because the closure
  of a single point is irreducible, and reduced by construction); the only
  non-automatic condition is the codimension.

  \paragraph{Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).} The Lean structure
  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor} bundles a point
  \texttt{point : X} together with a codimension-one witness, encoded via
  the Mathlib predicate
  \[
    \texttt{Order.coheight\ point\ =\ 1}
  \]
  on the specialisation preorder of the topological space underlying $X$.
  Concretely, Mathlib's preorder on \texttt{X.carrier} is defined by
  ``$x \leq y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x$'' (\ie\ $x \in \overline{\{y\}}$;
  see \texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Specialization} and
  \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}). Under this convention the generic
  point of an integral scheme is the unique \emph{maximal} element of the
  preorder, so for the generic point $\eta_Y$ of a prime divisor $Y$ the
  only point strictly above $\eta_Y$ is the generic point $\eta_X$ of $X$,
  and the chain $\eta_Y < \eta_X$ has length one, giving
  \texttt{Order.coheight\ $\eta_Y$\ =\ 1}. The DVR/integrality content of
  ``the closure of \texttt{point} with the reduced subscheme structure is
  integral'' is automatic from $\overline{\{\,\texttt{point}\,\}}$ being
  irreducible, so the Lean structure does not need a separate integrality
  field. The iter-172 placeholder \texttt{isCodim1AndIntegral : True} in
  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:84--90} is to be
  refined to \texttt{coheight : Order.coheight point = 1} (named field, type
  \texttt{Prop}) in the iter-173 prover step, retaining the same
  \texttt{point} field for downstream defeq.

  \paragraph{Reference predicates considered and rejected.}
  Two alternatives were considered and rejected at the iter-172
  lean-vs-blueprint-checker stage:
  \begin{itemize}
    \item \texttt{IsIntegral\ (X.closedSubschemeOfPoint\ x)}: rejected
      because Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships \emph{no}
      \texttt{closedSubschemeOfPoint} constructor. The closest available
      object is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData}
      (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf.Basic}), which packages
      a quasi-coherent ideal sheaf but does not produce a reduced induced
      subscheme on the closure of a point. Building that constructor is
      itself a Mathlib-upstream-scale project (Stacks tag 01J9 ``reduced
      induced scheme structure''), outside the scope of \texttt{RR.1}.
    \item A bundled \texttt{Scheme.IdealSheafData}-based prime-divisor
      carrier: rejected because the chapter only needs the generic-point
      witness for downstream order/principal-divisor consumption; carrying
      an \texttt{IdealSheafData} adds redundant data the chapter never
      uses.
  \end{itemize}
  The \texttt{Order.coheight}-based encoding is therefore the
  minimum-Mathlib-dependence pin compatible with the chapter's downstream
  needs.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}

\leanok
[Codim-$1$ cycle group / Weil divisor group of a scheme]
  \label{def:codim1_cycles}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor}
  \uses{def:prime_divisor}
  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "Weil divisor" under
  % condition $(*)$) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW.
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A \emph{Weil divisor} is an element of the free abelian
  % group $\operatorname{Div} X$ generated by the prime divisors. We write a
  % divisor as $D = \sum n_i Y_i$, where the $Y_i$ are prime divisors, the
  % $n_i$ are integers, and only finitely many $n_i$ are different from
  % zero. If all the $n_i \geq 0$, we say that $D$ is \emph{effective}."
  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (Weil divisor group).}
  Let $X$ be a Noetherian integral separated scheme that is regular in
  codimension one (i.e.\ $X$ satisfies $(*)$). The \emph{Weil divisor group}
  \[
    \Div(X) \;=\; \bigoplus_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
    \mathbb Z \cdot Y
  \]
  is the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of $X$
  (\Cref{def:prime_divisor}); an element $D = \sum n_i Y_i$ with finitely
  many nonzero coefficients $n_i \in \mathbb Z$ is called a \emph{Weil
  divisor}, and is \emph{effective} when all $n_i \geq 0$.

  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor X} is given for an arbitrary
  scheme \texttt{X : Scheme} as the formal-sum data type
  \texttt{X.PrimeDivisor $\to_0$ $\mathbb Z$}; integrality of $X$ is
  \emph{not} required to form the additive group $\Div(X)$ (it becomes
  necessary only at the order/principal-divisor layer where the function
  field appears). Hartshorne's $(*)$ is the standing convention in the
  prose but is not threaded into the typeclass arguments of this base
  declaration. See ``Standing hypothesis $(*)$ in the Lean encoding'' above
  for the per-layer typeclass discipline.
\end{definition}

\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (``Divisors on Curves'').}
  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over a field $k$ and let $P \in C$ be a
  closed point. The \emph{Weil divisor associated to $P$} is the element
  \[
    [P] \;:=\; 1 \cdot P \;\in\; \Div(C)
  \]
  i.e.\ the prime divisor $P$ with multiplicity one. The assignment is
  well-defined because the closed point $P$ is automatically a codimension-$1$
  integral closed subscheme of the $1$-dimensional integral scheme $C$.
  Conversely, every prime divisor on $C$ is of this form, so an arbitrary
  divisor on $C$ is a finite formal $\mathbb Z$-linear combination
  $\sum n_i [P_i]$ of closed points.
\end{definition}

\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (definition of degree on a
  nonsingular curve).}
  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
  $\bar k$. The \emph{degree} of a Weil divisor $D = \sum n_i [P_i] \in
  \Div(C)$ is the integer
  \[
    \deg(D) \;:=\; \sum_i n_i \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
  \]
  The definition uses that every closed point of $C$ has residue field
  $\bar k$ (so each prime divisor contributes degree one); over a general
  field $k$ the same definition with the residue-field-degree weight
  $\deg(D) := \sum_i n_i \, [\kappa(P_i) : k]$ recovers the
  \emph{geometric} degree, but the project's RR bridge needs only the
  $\bar k$ specialisation.
\end{definition}

3	\chapter{Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)}
4	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II \S6, pp.~130--137 +
7	% IV.1, pp.~294--296 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
8	% Stacks pointers (tag-level, not verbatim-quoted): 02RW (divisors),
9	% 02ME (order at a point on a regular 1-dimensional scheme),
10	% 0BE0 (degree of a divisor on a curve), 0BE3 (principal divisors have
11	% degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).
12	
13	\section{Setup and motivation}
14	
15	This chapter is the first of four sub-build chapters \texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4}
16	that close the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
17	\[
18	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
19	  \,\colon\,
20	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C / \bar k$ with
21	  $g(C) = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.}
22	\]
23	The bridge as proved in Hartshorne (Example~IV.1.3.5) chains together:
24	divisor of a closed point on a curve (\texttt{RR.1}, this chapter); the
25	Riemann--Roch dimension formula $\ell(D) = \deg D + 1$ for $\deg D \geq 0$ on
26	a genus-$0$ curve (\texttt{RR.2}); the global-sections argument identifying
27	$\dim_k H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ via $1$ and a non-constant function
28	(\texttt{RR.3}); the ``rational curve $\Rightarrow \cong \mathbb P^1$''
29	classification recovered as a degree-$1$ morphism into $\mathbb P^1$ via
30	\texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} (\texttt{RR.4}).
31	
32	Mathlib ships no \texttt{WeilDivisor} on a scheme. Adjacent pieces exist, but
33	none of them is the data structure we need: \texttt{MeromorphicOn.divisor}
34	(\texttt{Mathlib.Analysis.Meromorphic.Divisor}) is the order function of a
35	meromorphic function valued in a \emph{normed} field, not a formal sum of
36	codimension-$1$ cycles on a scheme; \texttt{CommRing.Pic}
37	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) is the ring-level Picard group of
38	invertible modules under tensor product, with no scheme-level analogue;
39	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap}
40	(\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}) packages rational
41	maps but not divisors. Consequently this chapter, and the three siblings, are
42	\emph{project-bespoke}: the only Mathlib tools they rely on are
43	discrete-valuation rings, the function field, and the ambient scheme API.
44	The audit grounding this scope decision is in
45	\texttt{analogies/rrbridge-survey.md}.
46	
47	This chapter establishes the formal-sum data type
48	$\Div(X) = \bigoplus_{\substack{Z \subset X \\ \codim 1}} \mathbb Z$ on a
49	Noetherian integral scheme $X$ satisfying Hartshorne's condition $(*)$, the
50	principal-divisor homomorphism $\div \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \Div(X)$ on a
51	curve, the degree map $\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z$ on a smooth proper
52	curve over an algebraically closed field, and the linear-equivalence
53	relation. The \emph{degree-zero of a principal divisor} statement
54	(\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}) is included for completeness; its proof
55	decomposes into a separate sub-build inside \texttt{RR.1} (it uses the
56	auxiliary lemma that any non-constant rational function on $C$ defines a
57	finite morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1$). The line-bundle construction $\mathcal
58	O_C(D)$ and the associated cohomology computation $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))$
59	live in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3}; the Riemann--Roch dimension
60	formula lives in \texttt{RR.2}; the genus-$0$ classification proper lives in
61	\texttt{RR.4}.
62	
63	Throughout this chapter, the standing convention is Hartshorne's condition
64	$(*)$:
65	
66	% SOURCE QUOTE: "$(\ast)$ $X$ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which
67	% is regular in codimension one."
68	\textit{Source: Hartshorne, II \S6, p.~130, the conditional $(*)$.}
69	\[
70	  (\ast)\qquad
71	  X \text{ is a noetherian integral separated scheme which is regular in
72	  codimension one.}
73	\]
74	For the degree map and for principal-divisor statements on a curve, we
75	additionally assume $X$ is a complete (i.e.\ proper) nonsingular curve over
76	an algebraically closed field $\bar k$, where Hartshorne's condition $(*)$ is
77	automatic.
78	
79	\paragraph{Standing hypothesis $(*)$ in the Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).}
80	Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) does not bundle Hartshorne's four-clause
81	$(\ast)$ as a single typeclass. The chapter pins $(*)$ to a \emph{layered}
82	typeclass set that the prover-agent threads through the Lean signatures in
83	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean}:
84	
85	\begin{itemize}
86	  \item \textbf{Basic formal-sum layer} (carrier of \Cref{def:prime_divisor} and
87	    \Cref{def:codim1_cycles}, the additive group $\Div(X)$, the degree
88	    \Cref{def:divisor_degree}, the degree-homomorphism
89	    \Cref{thm:divisor_degree_hom}, and \Cref{def:divisor_closed_point}): the
90	    only Mathlib typeclass invoked is \texttt{[IsIntegral X]}
91	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme} via
92	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Properties}). Integrality of $X$ already
93	    delivers \texttt{IrreducibleSpace X.carrier} (the carrier-level instance
94	    chain), so $X$ has a unique generic point and the function field
95	    \texttt{X.functionField} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField})
96	    is a field; separatedness and noetherianness are \emph{not} required for
97	    the bare formal-sum data type.
98	  \item \textbf{Order/principal layer} (\Cref{def:order_at_point},
99	    \Cref{def:principal_divisor}, \Cref{thm:principal_hom}): the Lean
100	    signature additionally threads
101	    \texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian X]}
102	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Noetherian}) and Hartshorne's
103	    regular-in-codimension-one clause. The latter has \emph{no} Mathlib
104	    typeclass shipping it directly; the chapter's pin is the project-side
105	    predicate ``for every $x \in X$ with $\codim_X \overline{\{x\}} = 1$ the
106	    stalk $\mathcal O_{X,x}$ is a discrete valuation ring'', encodable as the
107	    conjunction $\forall x,\;\Order.coheight x = 1 \Rightarrow
108	    \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing}\,(\mathcal O_{X,x})$. Iter-173+ may
109	    introduce a \texttt{Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne} predicate to
110	    abbreviate this; until then the order/principal lemmas thread the DVR
111	    hypothesis explicitly at each codim-1 point.
112	  \item \textbf{Curve layer} (\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}): the standing
113	    hypothesis tightens to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
114	    over $\bar k$ with explicit integrality witness, namely
115	    \begin{quote}
116	      \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
117	       (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
118	       \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
119	       \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]}
120	    \end{quote}
121	    matching the standing typeclass set used by the rest of the project's
122	    curve API (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus},
123	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity}). Hartshorne's $(*)$ is
124	    automatic for this layer (\texttt{[IsLocallyNoetherian]} from finite type
125	    over a field; regular-in-codim-1 from smoothness; separatedness from
126	    properness; integrality threaded).
127	\end{itemize}
128	
129	\noindent
130	The \texttt{[IsIntegral X]} hypothesis is the only one threaded through the
131	\emph{statement} of every Lean signature in
132	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean} that mentions a
133	function field, principal divisor, or order. The noetherian-and-DVR
134	content lives inside the proof bodies (sub-build), where the prover threads
135	the appropriate stronger hypothesis at the use site.
136	
137	\section{Codim-$1$ cycle group}
138	
139	\begin{definition}
140	
141	[Prime divisor of a scheme]
142	  \label{def:prime_divisor}
143	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor}
144	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "prime divisor" under
145	  % condition $(*)$) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
146	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (divisors).
147	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$. A \emph{prime
148	  % divisor} on $X$ is a closed integral subscheme $Y$ of codimension one."
149	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (definition of prime divisor).}
150	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. A \emph{prime divisor} on $X$ is a closed integral
151	  subscheme $Y \subset X$ of codimension one. Equivalently, by the standard
152	  generic-point correspondence (Hartshorne II \S6, p.~130, ``let $\eta \in
153	  Y$ be its generic point\ldots''), $Y$ is determined by its generic point
154	  $\eta \in X$: closed integral subschemes of codimension one of $X$ are in
155	  natural bijection with points $x \in X$ such that the closure
156	  $\overline{\{x\}} \subset X$ has codimension one. The closure
157	  $\overline{\{x\}}$, equipped with the \emph{reduced induced subscheme
158	  structure}, is automatically integral (irreducible, because the closure
159	  of a single point is irreducible, and reduced by construction); the only
160	  non-automatic condition is the codimension.
161	
162	  \paragraph{Lean encoding (iter-173 pin).} The Lean structure
163	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor} bundles a point
164	  \texttt{point : X} together with a codimension-one witness, encoded via
165	  the Mathlib predicate
166	  \[
167	    \texttt{Order.coheight\ point\ =\ 1}
168	  \]
169	  on the specialisation preorder of the topological space underlying $X$.
170	  Concretely, Mathlib's preorder on \texttt{X.carrier} is defined by
171	  ``$x \leq y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x$'' (\ie\ $x \in \overline{\{y\}}$;
172	  see \texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Specialization} and
173	  \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}). Under this convention the generic
174	  point of an integral scheme is the unique \emph{maximal} element of the
175	  preorder, so for the generic point $\eta_Y$ of a prime divisor $Y$ the
176	  only point strictly above $\eta_Y$ is the generic point $\eta_X$ of $X$,
177	  and the chain $\eta_Y < \eta_X$ has length one, giving
178	  \texttt{Order.coheight\ $\eta_Y$\ =\ 1}. The DVR/integrality content of
179	  ``the closure of \texttt{point} with the reduced subscheme structure is
180	  integral'' is automatic from $\overline{\{\,\texttt{point}\,\}}$ being
181	  irreducible, so the Lean structure does not need a separate integrality
182	  field. The iter-172 placeholder \texttt{isCodim1AndIntegral : True} in
183	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/WeilDivisor.lean:84--90} is to be
184	  refined to \texttt{coheight : Order.coheight point = 1} (named field, type
185	  \texttt{Prop}) in the iter-173 prover step, retaining the same
186	  \texttt{point} field for downstream defeq.
187	
188	  \paragraph{Reference predicates considered and rejected.}
189	  Two alternatives were considered and rejected at the iter-172
190	  lean-vs-blueprint-checker stage:
191	  \begin{itemize}
192	    \item \texttt{IsIntegral\ (X.closedSubschemeOfPoint\ x)}: rejected
193	      because Mathlib (\texttt{b80f227}) ships \emph{no}
194	      \texttt{closedSubschemeOfPoint} constructor. The closest available
195	      object is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IdealSheafData}
196	      (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.IdealSheaf.Basic}), which packages
197	      a quasi-coherent ideal sheaf but does not produce a reduced induced
198	      subscheme on the closure of a point. Building that constructor is
199	      itself a Mathlib-upstream-scale project (Stacks tag 01J9 ``reduced
200	      induced scheme structure''), outside the scope of \texttt{RR.1}.
201	    \item A bundled \texttt{Scheme.IdealSheafData}-based prime-divisor
202	      carrier: rejected because the chapter only needs the generic-point
203	      witness for downstream order/principal-divisor consumption; carrying
204	      an \texttt{IdealSheafData} adds redundant data the chapter never
205	      uses.
206	  \end{itemize}
207	  The \texttt{Order.coheight}-based encoding is therefore the
208	  minimum-Mathlib-dependence pin compatible with the chapter's downstream
209	  needs.
210	\end{definition}
211	
212	\begin{definition}
213	
214	\leanok
215	[Codim-$1$ cycle group / Weil divisor group of a scheme]
216	  \label{def:codim1_cycles}
217	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor}
218	  \uses{def:prime_divisor}
219	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (definition of "Weil divisor" under
220	  % condition $(*)$) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
221	  % PDF page 147). Stacks pointer: tag 02RW.
222	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A \emph{Weil divisor} is an element of the free abelian
223	  % group $\operatorname{Div} X$ generated by the prime divisors. We write a
224	  % divisor as $D = \sum n_i Y_i$, where the $Y_i$ are prime divisors, the
225	  % $n_i$ are integers, and only finitely many $n_i$ are different from
226	  % zero. If all the $n_i \geq 0$, we say that $D$ is \emph{effective}."
227	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~130 (Weil divisor group).}
228	  Let $X$ be a Noetherian integral separated scheme that is regular in
229	  codimension one (i.e.\ $X$ satisfies $(*)$). The \emph{Weil divisor group}
230	  \[
231	    \Div(X) \;=\; \bigoplus_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
232	    \mathbb Z \cdot Y
233	  \]
234	  is the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of $X$
235	  (\Cref{def:prime_divisor}); an element $D = \sum n_i Y_i$ with finitely
236	  many nonzero coefficients $n_i \in \mathbb Z$ is called a \emph{Weil
237	  divisor}, and is \emph{effective} when all $n_i \geq 0$.
238	
239	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
240	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor X} is given for an arbitrary
241	  scheme \texttt{X : Scheme} as the formal-sum data type
242	  \texttt{X.PrimeDivisor $\to_0$ $\mathbb Z$}; integrality of $X$ is
243	  \emph{not} required to form the additive group $\Div(X)$ (it becomes
244	  necessary only at the order/principal-divisor layer where the function
245	  field appears). Hartshorne's $(*)$ is the standing convention in the
246	  prose but is not threaded into the typeclass arguments of this base
247	  declaration. See ``Standing hypothesis $(*)$ in the Lean encoding'' above
248	  for the per-layer typeclass discipline.
249	\end{definition}
250	
251	\section{Order of a rational function at a prime divisor}
252	
253	\begin{definition}
254	
255	\leanok
256	[Order of a rational function at a prime divisor]
257	  \label{def:order_at_point}
258	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order}
259	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
260	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130--131 (valuation $v_Y$ of a prime
261	  % divisor; "zero/pole of $f$ along $Y$") (read from
262	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
263	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02ME.
264	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If $Y$ is a prime divisor on $X$, let $\eta \in Y$ be its
265	  % generic point. Then the local ring $\mathcal O_{\eta, X}$ of $X$ at
266	  % $\eta$ is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field $K$, the
267	  % function field of $X$. We call the corresponding discrete valuation
268	  % $v_Y$ the \emph{valuation of $Y$}."
269	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let $f \in K^\ast$ be any nonzero rational function
270	  % on $X$. Then $v_Y(f)$ is an integer. If it is positive, we say $f$ has
271	  % a \emph{zero} along $Y$, of that order; if it is negative, we say $f$
272	  % has a \emph{pole} along $Y$, of order $-v_Y(f)$."
273	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation $v_Y$).}
274	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. For a prime divisor $Y \subset X$ with generic
275	  point $\eta \in Y$, the local ring $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$ is a discrete
276	  valuation ring (by regularity in codimension one) with quotient field the
277	  function field $K(X)$. For a nonzero rational function $f \in K(X)^{\times}$
278	  the \emph{order of $f$ along $Y$} is the integer
279	  \[
280	    \ord_Y(f) \;:=\; v_Y(f) \;\in\; \mathbb Z,
281	  \]
282	  where $v_Y$ is the normalised discrete valuation associated to
283	  $\mathcal O_{X, \eta}$. If $\ord_Y(f) > 0$, $f$ has a \emph{zero of
284	  order $\ord_Y(f)$ along $Y$}; if $\ord_Y(f) < 0$, $f$ has a \emph{pole
285	  of order $-\ord_Y(f)$ along $Y$}.
286	
287	  Specialisation to a curve. If $C$ is a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$
288	  and $P \in C$ is a closed point, then $P$ is automatically a prime
289	  divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the $1$-dimensional
290	  scheme $C$); the local ring $\mathcal O_{C, P}$ is a discrete valuation
291	  ring by smoothness, and $\ord_P(f) = v_P(f)$ is the standard DVR
292	  valuation of $f$ at $P$.
293	\end{definition}
294	
295	\section{Divisor of a closed point on a curve}
296	
297	\begin{definition}
298	
299	\leanok
300	[Divisor of a closed point on a curve]
301	  \label{def:divisor_closed_point}
302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint}
303	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
304	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
305	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154):
306	  % "A prime divisor is just a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can
307	  % be written $D = \sum n_i P_i$, where the $P_i$ are closed points, and
308	  % $n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
309	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now we come to the study of divisors on curves. If $X$
310	  % is a nonsingular curve, then $X$ satisfies the condition $(\ast)$ used
311	  % above, so we can talk about divisors on $X$. A prime divisor is just
312	  % a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can be written $D = \sum n_i
313	  % P_i$, where the $P_i$ are closed points, and $n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
314	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (``Divisors on Curves'').}
315	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over a field $k$ and let $P \in C$ be a
316	  closed point. The \emph{Weil divisor associated to $P$} is the element
317	  \[
318	    [P] \;:=\; 1 \cdot P \;\in\; \Div(C)
319	  \]
320	  i.e.\ the prime divisor $P$ with multiplicity one. The assignment is
321	  well-defined because the closed point $P$ is automatically a codimension-$1$
322	  integral closed subscheme of the $1$-dimensional integral scheme $C$.
323	  Conversely, every prime divisor on $C$ is of this form, so an arbitrary
324	  divisor on $C$ is a finite formal $\mathbb Z$-linear combination
325	  $\sum n_i [P_i]$ of closed points.
326	
327	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean signature
328	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint} accepts a
329	  scheme \texttt{C : Scheme.\{u\}}, a point \texttt{P : C}, and a hypothesis
330	  \texttt{IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C)}. It does \emph{not} typeclass-pin
331	  ``smooth proper curve over $\bar k$'' at the carrier level; the
332	  well-definedness of \texttt{ofClosedPoint} as a \texttt{PrimeDivisor}
333	  promotion goes through a separate argument that, on a one-dimensional
334	  integral scheme, \texttt{IsClosed \{P\}} implies
335	  \texttt{Order.coheight P = 1}. The prover-agent threads the appropriate
336	  curve-level typeclasses inside the proof body, not in the signature; the
337	  blueprint-level statement is the one in the prose above.
338	\end{definition}
339	
340	\section{Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base}
341	
342	\begin{definition}
343	
344	\leanok
345	[Degree of a divisor on a curve over $\bar k$]
346	  \label{def:divisor_degree}
347	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree}
348	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:divisor_closed_point}
349	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
350	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
351	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE0.
352	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We define the \emph{degree} of $D$ to be $\sum n_i$."
353	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (definition of degree on a
354	  nonsingular curve).}
355	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
356	  $\bar k$. The \emph{degree} of a Weil divisor $D = \sum n_i [P_i] \in
357	  \Div(C)$ is the integer
358	  \[
359	    \deg(D) \;:=\; \sum_i n_i \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
360	  \]
361	  The definition uses that every closed point of $C$ has residue field
362	  $\bar k$ (so each prime divisor contributes degree one); over a general
363	  field $k$ the same definition with the residue-field-degree weight
364	  $\deg(D) := \sum_i n_i \, [\kappa(P_i) : k]$ recovers the
365	  \emph{geometric} degree, but the project's RR bridge needs only the
366	  $\bar k$ specialisation.
367	
368	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
369	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree} is given for an
370	  arbitrary scheme \texttt{X : Scheme.\{u\}} as the literal sum of
371	  coefficients via \texttt{Finsupp.sum D (fun \_ n => n)}; the typeclass
372	  set ``smooth proper curve over $\bar k$'' is \emph{not} pinned on the
373	  base declaration. Mathematically this assigns to every Weil divisor
374	  $D \in \Div(X)$ on any scheme $X$ the integer-valued sum of its
375	  multiplicities; for a smooth proper curve over $\bar k$ this coincides
376	  with the curve-theoretic degree of the prose (every closed point has
377	  residue field $\bar k$, so the residue-field-degree weight is identically
378	  one). The chapter's prose pins the curve hypothesis for clarity and to
379	  flag that, on a scheme with non-trivial residue-field extensions, the
380	  bare-sum signature does \emph{not} compute the geometric degree; the
381	  curve hypothesis is threaded at the call sites that consume
382	  \texttt{degree} for arithmetic statements
383	  (\Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero}, \texttt{RR.2}--\texttt{RR.4}).
384	\end{definition}
385	
386	\begin{theorem}
387	
388	\leanok
389	[The degree map is a group homomorphism]
390	  \label{thm:divisor_degree_hom}
391	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_hom}
392	  \uses{def:divisor_degree}
393	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
394	  $\bar k$. The degree map of \Cref{def:divisor_degree} is a group
395	  homomorphism
396	  \[
397	    \deg \colon \Div(C) \longrightarrow \mathbb Z,
398	    \qquad
399	    \deg(D_1 + D_2) \;=\; \deg(D_1) + \deg(D_2),
400	    \qquad
401	    \deg(-D) \;=\; -\deg(D).
402	  \]
403	\end{theorem}
404	
405	\begin{proof}
406	  \uses{def:divisor_degree}
407	  Immediate from the definition: $\Div(C)$ is the free abelian group on the
408	  set of closed points of $C$, and $\deg$ is the unique group homomorphism
409	  sending each generator $[P]$ to $1 \in \mathbb Z$ (equivalently: the sum
410	  of the multiplicities). Both additivity and negation thus reduce to the
411	  corresponding identities for finite sums of integers.
412	\end{proof}
413	
414	\section{Principal divisors}
415	
416	\begin{definition}
417	
418	\leanok
419	[Principal divisor of a rational function]
420	  \label{def:principal_divisor}
421	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal}
422	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:order_at_point}
423	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Lemma~6.1 + the definition of $\operatorname{div}(f) = (f)$,
424	  % p.~131 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
425	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma 6.1. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$, and let $f \in K^\ast$
426	  % be a nonzero function on $X$. Then $v_Y(f) = 0$ for all except finitely
427	  % many prime divisors $Y$."
428	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$ and let $f \in K^\ast$.
429	  % We define the \emph{divisor of $f$}, denoted $(f)$, by
430	  % $(f) = \sum v_Y(f) \cdot Y$, where the sum is taken over all prime
431	  % divisors $Y$ of $X$. By the lemma, this is a finite sum, hence is a
432	  % divisor. Any divisor which is equal to the divisor of a function is
433	  % called a \emph{principal divisor}."
434	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Lemma~6.1 and the following definition,
435	  p.~131.}
436	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. For a nonzero rational function $f \in K(X)^{\times}$,
437	  Hartshorne's Lemma~6.1 asserts that $\ord_Y(f) = 0$ for all but finitely
438	  many prime divisors $Y$ on $X$; hence the formal sum
439	  \[
440	    \div(f) \;:=\; \sum_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
441	    \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y \;\in\; \Div(X)
442	  \]
443	  has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor, called the
444	  \emph{principal divisor of $f$}. On a smooth proper curve $C$ over $\bar k$
445	  the same definition specialises to
446	  $\div(f) = \sum_{P \in C \text{ closed}} \ord_P(f) \cdot [P]$, the formal
447	  sum over closed points.
448	\end{definition}
449	
450	\begin{theorem}
451	
452	\leanok
453	[The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism]
454	  \label{thm:principal_hom}
455	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_hom}
456	  \uses{def:principal_divisor, def:order_at_point}
457	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, the paragraph after the principal-divisor
458	  % definition, p.~131 (read from
459	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
460	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Note that if $f, g \in K^\ast$, then $(f/g) = (f) - (g)$
461	  % because of the properties of valuations. Therefore sending a function
462	  % $f$ to its divisor $(f)$ gives a homomorphism of the multiplicative
463	  % group $K^\ast$ to the additive group $\operatorname{Div} X$, and the
464	  % image, which consists of the principal divisors, is a subgroup of
465	  % $\operatorname{Div} X$."
466	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (group-homomorphism remark).}
467	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. The principal-divisor map
468	  $\div \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \Div(X)$ is a group homomorphism from the
469	  multiplicative group of nonzero rational functions to the additive group
470	  of Weil divisors. Concretely
471	  \[
472	    \div(fg) \;=\; \div(f) + \div(g),
473	    \qquad
474	    \div(f^{-1}) \;=\; -\div(f),
475	    \qquad
476	    \div(1) \;=\; 0.
477	  \]
478	\end{theorem}
479	
480	\begin{proof}
481	  \uses{def:principal_divisor, def:order_at_point}
482	  Each prime divisor $Y$ on $X$ contributes a discrete valuation $v_Y$ on
483	  $K(X)$ (\Cref{def:order_at_point}); by the standard DVR axioms,
484	  $v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)$ and $v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)$ for $f, g \in
485	  K(X)^{\times}$, and $v_Y(1) = 0$. Summing these identities over all prime
486	  divisors of $X$ (a sum that has finite support on each factor by
487	  \Cref{def:principal_divisor}, so the support of the sum is the union
488	  of the two finite supports and remains finite) gives the three displayed
489	  identities.
490	\end{proof}
491	
492	\begin{theorem}
493	
494	\leanok
495	[Principal divisors have degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve]
496	  \label{thm:principal_deg_zero}
497	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero}
498	  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:principal_divisor, thm:principal_hom}
499	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138 (read from
500	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 155).
501	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE3.
502	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.10. \emph{A principal divisor on a complete
503	  % nonsingular curve $X$ has degree zero. Consequently the degree function
504	  % induces a surjective homomorphism $\deg \colon \operatorname{Cl} X \to
505	  % \mathbf Z$.}"
506	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138.}
507	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
508	  $\bar k$. For every nonzero rational function $f \in K(C)^{\times}$ the
509	  associated principal divisor has degree zero:
510	  \[
511	    \deg(\div(f)) \;=\; 0 \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
512	  \]
513	\end{theorem}
514	
515	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Let $f \in K(X)^\ast$. If $f \in k$, then
516	% $(f) = 0$, so there is nothing to prove. If $f \notin k$, then the
517	% inclusion of fields $k(f) \subset K(X)$ induces a finite morphism
518	% $\varphi \colon X \to \mathbf P^1$. It is a morphism by (I, 6.12). We
519	% have $(f) = \varphi^\ast(\{0\} - \{\infty\})$. Since $\{0\} - \{\infty\}$
520	% is a divisor of degree 0 on $\mathbf P^1$, we conclude that $(f)$ has
521	% degree 0 on $X$."
522	\begin{proof}
523	  \uses{def:divisor_degree, def:principal_divisor, thm:principal_hom}
524	  Following Hartshorne's Corollary~6.10. If $f \in \bar k \subset K(C)$ is
525	  a constant scalar, then $f$ has no zeros or poles, so $\div(f) = 0$ and
526	  $\deg(\div(f)) = 0$. Otherwise the inclusion of fields
527	  $\bar k(f) \subset K(C)$ exhibits $K(C)$ as a finite extension of the
528	  rational function field $\bar k(f) \cong \bar k(t)$, so the corresponding
529	  morphism $\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ is finite (by the
530	  smooth-proper-curve / function-field correspondence on which a curve is
531	  determined by its function field, classical I.6.12). The principal
532	  divisor $\div(f)$ equals the pullback $\varphi^{*}([0] - [\infty])$
533	  along $\varphi$ of the degree-$0$ divisor $[0] - [\infty]$ on
534	  $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$; since pullback along a finite morphism
535	  multiplies degrees by $\deg(\varphi)$, we conclude
536	  $\deg(\div(f)) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg([0] - [\infty]) = \deg(\varphi)
537	  \cdot 0 = 0$.
538	
539	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The two auxiliary statements ``the inclusion
540	  $\bar k(f) \subset K(C)$ defines a finite morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1$''
541	  and ``pullback along a finite morphism of curves multiplies degrees by
542	  the degree of the morphism'' (Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.9, the
543	  multiplicativity of degree) are themselves separable sub-lemmas of
544	  \texttt{RR.1}; they are stated here for completeness and slotted to be
545	  closed in a follow-up iteration. The signature
546	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal\_degree\_zero}
547	  is recorded as the public face of this theorem and is depended on by
548	  the \texttt{RR.3}/\texttt{RR.4} chain.
549	\end{proof}
550	
551	\section{Divisor class group}
552	
553	We follow Hartshorne II.6 (pp.~131--132) and define the divisor class group
554	of a curve as the cokernel of the principal-divisor homomorphism. This is
555	the \emph{Picard group} of $C$ in the curve-specific sense; the line-bundle
556	interpretation $\Cl(C) \cong \Pic(C)$ requires the construction of
557	$\mathcal O_C(D)$, which lives in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3} and is
558	out of scope here.
559	
560	\begin{definition}
561	
562	\leanok
563	[Linear equivalence of divisors]
564	  \label{def:linear_equivalence}
565	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.LinearEquivalence}
566	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:principal_divisor, thm:principal_hom}
567	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~131 (definition of linear equivalence
568	  % and of $\operatorname{Cl} X$) (read from
569	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
570	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let $X$ satisfy $(\ast)$. Two divisors $D$
571	  % and $D'$ are said to be \emph{linearly equivalent}, written $D \sim D'$,
572	  % if $D - D'$ is a principal divisor. The group $\operatorname{Div} X$ of
573	  % all divisors divided by the subgroup of principal divisors is called
574	  % the \emph{divisor class group} of $X$, and is denoted by
575	  % $\operatorname{Cl} X$."
576	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (linear equivalence and
577	  $\operatorname{Cl} X$).}
578	  Let $X$ satisfy $(*)$. Two Weil divisors $D, D' \in \Div(X)$ are
579	  \emph{linearly equivalent}, written $D \sim D'$, if and only if there
580	  exists $f \in K(X)^{\times}$ with
581	  \[
582	    D - D' \;=\; \div(f) \;\in\; \Div(X).
583	  \]
584	  The relation $\sim$ is an equivalence relation on $\Div(X)$ (the
585	  reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity all transport from the analogous
586	  identities $\div(1) = 0$, $\div(f^{-1}) = -\div(f)$, $\div(fg) =
587	  \div(f) + \div(g)$ via \Cref{thm:principal_hom}); the corresponding
588	  quotient group $\Cl(X) := \Div(X) / \mathrm{im}(\div)$ is the
589	  \emph{divisor class group} of $X$. On a smooth proper curve $C$ over
590	  $\bar k$, \Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero} implies that the degree map
591	  $\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z$ descends to a homomorphism
592	  $\deg \colon \Cl(C) \to \mathbb Z$.
593	\end{definition}
594	
595	\section{Out of scope}
596	
597	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.1} and
598	are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters:
599	
600	\begin{itemize}
601	  \item The line-bundle construction $\mathcal O_C(D)$ associated to a Weil
602	    divisor $D$, the calculation of its global sections, and the
603	    identification $\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')$ when
604	    $D \sim D'$, lives in \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
605	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added).
606	  \item The Riemann--Roch dimension formula
607	    $\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g$ and its genus-$0$ specialisation
608	    $\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1$ for $\deg(D) \geq 0$ live in \texttt{RR.2}
609	    (sibling chapter \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RRFormula.tex}, to be added).
610	  \item The classification ``a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
611	    curve over $\bar k$ with $g = 0$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$''
612	    (Hartshorne Example~IV.1.3.5) is the headline of \texttt{RR.4}
613	    (sibling chapter \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added)
614	    and assembles \texttt{RR.1}/\texttt{RR.2}/\texttt{RR.3} into the
615	    \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} morphism into $\mathbb P^1$ together
616	    with its degree-$1$ identification.
617	  \item \emph{Cartier divisors} (locally principal divisors on a possibly
618	    singular scheme) are out of scope: the project's curves are smooth, so
619	    Weil and Cartier divisors coincide, and the simpler Weil form suffices.
620	    A future Mathlib upstream PR could introduce the Cartier formalism.
621	  \item \emph{Higher-codimension cycles} (the Chow group
622	    $\bigoplus_i \mathrm{CH}^i(X)$) are out of scope: this chapter is
623	    strictly curve-focused.
624	  \item The proof of \Cref{thm:principal_deg_zero} factors through two
625	    auxiliary statements (``finite morphism induced by a non-constant
626	    rational function'' and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite
627	    pullback'', Hartshorne II.6.9) which are themselves separable
628	    sub-lemmas of \texttt{RR.1}; they are flagged in the proof body but
629	    deliberately not unfolded as standalone declarations here.
630	\end{itemize}
